description: Role of Probability in Engineering Planning and Design; Introduction to Probability Theory; Analyrical Models for Random Phenomena; Functions of Random Variables; Multivariate Probability Distributions; Stochastic Models and Chapman-Kolmogorov Equations; Birth-Death Processes; use of Z-Transforms; Decision Theory; Fame Theory; Probabilistic Models for Inventory Control; Queuing Theory and Scheduling Applicaitons of Probabilistic Models to Chemical Process Engineering Design and Planning, Statistical Quality Control; Statistical Process Analysis, Reliability Analysis.

title: Thermodynamics of Polymeric Systems
description: 1. Constitution and Architecture of Polymers 2. Glassy, Crystalline and Molten States of Polymers: Thermodynamics of Melting of Polymers, Kinetics of Crystallisation (Avrami Equation), Thermodynamics of Glass Transition, Free Volume theory. 3. Polymer Chain Conformations: Random flight model for polymer, The Gaussian Chain, Chain Conformation under an External Field, Excluded Volume Effect and Theta Condition, Rotational Isomeric States, Scaling Concept. 4. Mechanical Behaviour of Polymers: Rubber Elasticity, Network Model. 5. Viscosity of Polymer Melts and Solution: Viscosity of Concentrated Solutions and Melts, Effect of Branching on Viscosity, Elasticity and Viscoelasticity, Maxwell Model for Viscoelasticity. 6. Thermodynamics of Polymer Solutions: Dilute, Semidilute and Concentrated Solutions, Osmotic Pressure â€“ MW Measurement, Zimm Diagram â€“ MW Measurement, Viscosity of Dilute Solutions â€“ MW Measurement, Screening Effect, Flory-Huggins Theory, Phase Equilibria in Polymer Solutions, Critical Fluctuations and Spinodal Decomposition, Phase Behaviour of Block Copolymers.

title: Polymer Science and Engineering
description: 1. Constitution and Architecture of Polymers: Chemical Structure of Polymers (Polymer Nomenclature); Molecular Weight Distributions (PDI, etc.); Chain Architecture (Linear/Branched, Tacticity, Isomerism) 2. Polymer Synthesis: Step Growth Polymerisation: Chain Growth Polymerisation; Emulsion Polymerisatio.; Ionic Polymerisation; Synthesis of Copolymers and Stereo-regular Polymers. 3. Glassy, Crystalline and Molten States of Polymers: Meaning of Crystallinity, Fraction Crystallinity; Meaning of Tg, Tg vs. Mn; Introduction to Free Volume Concepts 4. Polymer Rheology and Viscoelasticity; Rheology (Power Law, etc. Models): Boltzmanâ€™s Superposition Principle; Viscoelasticity (Maxwell, Kelvin, etc., Models, Dynamic Mechanical Analysis); Time-Temperature Superposition; Dielectric Analysis

title: Chemical Engineering Lab IV
description: (Focus: Process Control, Unit Operations) Experiments on Residence time distribution, continuous distillation, Dynamic process modeling, identification of transfer functions, P /PI control, Temperature measurement and signal conversion, Control valve characteristics, Fixed bed reactor, drying

title: Safety in Chemical Industry
description: Concepts and definition, safety culture, Storage of dangerous materials. Plant layout. Safety systems. Technology and process selection. Scale of disaster. Vapour Cloud Explosions. Control of toxic chemicals. Runway reactions. Relief Systems. Risk and hazard management. Safety versus production. Risk assessment and analysis. Hazard models and risk data. Identification, minimisation and analysis of hazard. Tackling disasters. Plan for emergency. Risk management routines. Emergency shut down systems. Role of computers in safety. Prevention of hazard. Human element. Technology and process selection. Design of safety.

title: SLP-IDP
description: The only course in the chemical engineering curriculum that offers professional experience at the undergraduate level along with mentoring from institute mentors is SLP-IDP. Because the corporation was required by the agreement to supply all relevant information under a confidentially agreement signed by us, it gave an experience similar to an internship but in a more organised manner. Additionally, because SLP-IDP is a group project, as opposed to other SLP projects, it will generate an environment that is akin to a working one.

title: Chemical Process Design
description: Process Design and Development: General Design Considerations; The Hierarchy of Chemical Process Design; The Nature of Process Synthesis and Analysis; Reactor networks in process flowsheets: Attainable region Separation systems in process flowsheets: multicomponent distillation for ideal and non-ideal systems, distillation column sequences, heat integration in distillation columns Heat exchange networks synthesis and utilities: Energy targets Introduction to optimization approaches to optimal design, role of simulations in process design, Design under uncertainty and failure tolerance, Engineering around variations, Introduction to process integration

title: Process Design Project
description: Process and mechanical design calculations for process equipment; Numerical studies in reactor design; Design and analysis of separation equipment.

timetable.professor: Madhu Vinjamur, Sandip Salil Roy, Sujit Suresh Jogwar, Yogendra Narayan Shastri, Hemant Jayantkumar Nanavati, Sanjay Mahajani

title: Design Lab I
description: Steady-state simulation of flow sheets; Optimization and costing in flow sheets; Design and analysis of control systems; Simulations using commercial simulators (e.g. ASPEN, HYSYS), Computational Fluid Dynamics, Molecular modeling.

title: Colloid & Interfacial Engineering
description: Phenomenology of colloidal materials. Brownian diffusion. Long range van der Waals forces. Double layer forces and short range forces. DLVO theory of stability of lyphobic colloids. Electrokinetic phenomena. Association Colloids. Interfacial tension. Wetting and contact angle. Capillary hydrostatics. Interfacial rheology. Interfacial stability. Some selected applications of principles of Colloid and Interface Science in Detergents, Personal Products, Pharmaceutical, Food, Textile, Paint and Petroleum Industries.

title: Process safety and Risk Management
description: • Basic Concepts of Process Hazards and Risk • Principles of Process Safety and Risk Management • Safety Considerations during Process Conceptualization, Route Selection and Development • Reading and Designing P&IDs: Process Safety Implications • Determinants of Process Hazards; Process Hazard Identification Methodologies • Nature of Major Chemical Accidents; Accident Initiating Events; Accident Propagating Factors • Characteristics of Fires, Explosions and Toxic Releases • Plant Layout Design and Storage of Hazardous Materials; Methods of Mitigation of Hazards • Designing for Safety: Relief Design, Inerting, Purging, Concepts of Inherent Safety • Concept of Process Risk and its Management; Process Risk Acceptance Criteria; Concepts of Individual and Societal Risk • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • Accident Consequence Modelling; Continuous and Instantaneous Discharge of Hazardous Materials; Plume and Puff Dispersion Models; Neutrally Buoyant and Heavy Gas Dispersion Phenomena Modelling of Vapour Cloud Explosions Modelling of Fireballs, Pool fires and Jet fires, Flash Fires Accident Effect Modelling: Probit Analysis Fault and Event Tree Analysis Probability Theory in Chemical Process Risk Assessment Reliability Theory and Modelling; Static Reliability Modelling; Effect of Maintenance on Reliability Markov Chain Modelling of Faults in Engineering Systems Layer of Protection Analysis Qualitative and Quantitative Risk Assessment: Methodology and Case Studies Use of Software for Process Hazard Identification and Risk Analysis Analysis of Reactive Hazards Human and Organizational Factors in Process Safety Management Emergency Planning Case Histories and Lessons of Select Major Process Accidents

title: Lattice Boltzmann Method for Fluid Flows
description: Kinetic theory and Cellular automata Equilibrium distribution function Boltzmann’s H theorem Discretized Boltzmann equation and BGK Collision operator Discrete velocity sets Lattice isotropy considerations Collision and Streaming Analysis of lattice Boltzmann equations Chapman-Enskog analysis Recursive representation of lattice Boltzmann equation Initial and Boundary conditions Periodic, no-slip, slip, inlet, outlet boundaries Error analysis of boundary conditions Choice of simulation parameters Two-phase flow models Pseudo-potential model Phase-field model Chromodynamic model

title: Practice School- Chemical Engineering Industrial Project
description: The course will require the student to spend approximately 5 to 6 months in a corechemical industry. He / She will work onaproject closely mentored by one / two facultymembers from IIT Bombay Chemical Engineering department and also an industrymentor (typically a PhD). The course will be restricted to a few students at top chemicalcompanies. Total of 100 man days of actual industry exposure would result.The culmination of the course will result in a short report and presentation. Periodicmonitoring would be via monthly presentations by the student and faculty visits to the company. The projects would be carefully chosen to challenge the top students of the department. Assessment would be via the quality of the end-of-course presentation but also via vivas, quizzes and detailed feedback rubrics from the industry mentors.

timetable.professor: Madhu Vinjamur, Rahul Nabar, Sanjay Mahajani

title: Computational Methods in Catalysis
description: • Introduction to molecular modeling and computational chemistry • Molecular mechanics: Definition of force fields, force field parameterization, ensembles, molecular dynamics algorithm, limitations of molecular mechanics • Electronic structure calculations/ ab initio methods, Postulates of quantum mechanics, Schrödinger equation, Born – Oppenheimer approximation, Solution for the hydrogen atom and approximate solution for helium, Basis sets, Slater orbitals, Gaussian orbitals, SCF Algorithm, Hartree – Fock method • Post Hartree – Fock methods: DFT, Configuration Interaction, Moller Plesset Perturbation theory, Coupled Cluster Theory • Density functional theory: Origin and formulation of DFT, Hohenberg – Kohn theorem, Kohn – Sham formulation, DFT functionals: Local density approximation and generalized gradient approximation • Modelling of periodic system: DFT for simple solids – plane waves, kpoints, pseudopotentials, DFT calculations – geometry optimization, transition state search, vibrational frequency calculations • ab initio molecular dynamics and Car-Parrinello molecular dynamics: Car-Parrinello scheme and its implementation, Biasing methods – Metadynamics • Reaction kinetics and thermochemistry: From DFT to an engineering kinetic model – Statistical mechanics, Transition state theory, Reaction kinetics

title: Management of Chemical Projects
description: 1. Identification, development, formulation and economic evaluation of chemical projects 2. Scaleup, process flow diagrams, safety, basic and detailed engineering of chemical plants 3. Execution of the projects covering procurement, construction, coordination, statutory approvals and overall project management 4. Commissioning of plants, market entry and post project review

title: Advanced Transport Phenomena
description: Vector and tensor algebra. Kinematics: Motion, streamlines, pathlines and streaklines.Governing equations of fluid mechanics: Equation of continuity, Momentum balance, Stress tensor, constitutive equations, Boundary conditions. Unidirectional flows: steady and unsteady; Two-dimensional flows: Stream function. Limiting cases: Creeping flow, Inviscid flow, Boundary layer theory, Turbulent flow: Transition to turbulence, Turbulence models. Macroscopic balances. Governing equations of heat transfer: Energy balance, Constitutive equations, Boundary conditions, Conduction, Forced convection, Natural convection. Radiation, Macroscopic balances. Concentrations, velocities and mass fluxes: Governing equations of mass transfer: Species mass balance, Constitutive equations, Boundary conditions. Complete solutions, Diffusion in gases, Diffusion and reaction, Forced convection, Simultaneous heat and mass transfer.

title: Mathematical and Statistical Methods in Chemical Engineering
description: Linear Algebra: Linear spaces, Vector spaces, Function spaces, Linear operator theory, self-adjoint operators, Eigenvalues and eigenvectors-eigenfunctions, Cayley-Hamilton theorem, Polynomials and functions defined on matrices, Similarity transformations, Jordan forms, quadratic forms, Strum-Liouville equations and solution of boundary value problems, Finite difference equations, Difference operators, Generating functions and Z-transforms. Nonlinear Ordinary Differential Equations: Autonomous/ nonautonomous systems of odes, Phase plane analysis, Limit cycle and bifurcation, regular and singular perturbation techniques, Poincare and Liapunov stability criteria, Chaos, Differential-Algebraic equations. Graph theory: Classification of graphs, matrix representation of graphs, Analysis of trees, directed graphs and networks. Partial Differential Equations: Partial differential operators, First order partial differential equations, Method of characteristics, Classification of the second order partial differential equations and boundary conditions, Method of separation of variables, Similarity solutions, Green?s functions, Laplace and Fourier transforms. Statistical methods: Random variables, Probability distributions, Stochastic Processes, Random numbers and their generation, Monte-Carlo simulation, Response surface methodology, First and second order orthogonal factorial design, Regression analysis, Least square estimation of regression parameters, Lack of fit.

title: Optimization
description: Introduction to Process Optimization; Formulation of Various Process Optimization Problems and their Classification; Basic Concepts of Optimization-Convex and Concave Functions, Necessary and sufficient conditions for Stationary Points; Optimization of one-dimensional Functions; Unconstrained Multivariable Optimization- Direct Search Methods. Indirect First Order and Second Order Methods; Linear Programming and its Applications; Constrained Multivariable Optimization-Necessary and Sufficient Conditions for Constrained Optimum, Quadratic Programming, Generalized Reduced Gradient Method, Successive Linear and Quadratic Programming; Optimization of Staged and Discrete Processes, Dynamic Programming, Integer and Mixed Integer Programming.

title: Process Plant Simulation
description: Introduction to Process Plant Simulation, Various Approaches to Plant Simulation, Steady-State Sequential Introduction to process model building and simulation; Various approaches to plant simulation; Flowsheet topology representation methods and recycle detection algorithms; Steady-state sequential modular simulation techniques, concepts of partitioning and tearing; Recycle convergence algorithms; Equation oriented simulation techniques; Solution methods for sparse systems; Simultaneous modular simulation techniques; Steady state commercial simulation packages and their features; Problem solving using selected commercial simulators; Thermodynamic Procedures in Process Simulators; Introduction to Dynamic Simulation; Commercial Dynamic Simulators and their applications.

title: Advanced Reaction Engineering
description: Homogeneous reactor design and analysis-I: Ideal reactors, Review of isothermal design for batch, semi-batch and flow reactors, Multiple reactions and reaction networks: Yield-selectivity concepts, Wei-Prater analysis for first order networks, reaction networks of general order, Reactor energy balance and its applications to reactor design and analysis. Homogeneous reactor design and analysis-II: Non-ideal reactors- Review of the basic concepts of residence time distributions, single parameter models for real reactor behavior, Macromixing and micromixing, segregated flow model and Zweitering`s analysis of maximum mixedness, IEM and other models for micromixing. Heterogeneous reactors-I: Gas-solid systems- Review of kinetics of gas-solid catalytic reactions with and without diffusion limitations, Reactor design for fixed and fluidized bed reactors, Selected case studies, Non-catalytic gas-solid reactions: review of kinetics; reactor design case studies.Heterogeneous reactors-II: Gas-liquid systems- Basic theories of mass transfer with chemical reaction model systems and model reactors, Reactor design for mechanically agitated and bubble column reactors. Selected case studies.

title: Advanced Thermodynamics
description: Review of Basic Postulates, Maxwell`s relations, Legendre Transformation, Pure Component properties, Theory of corresponding states, real fluids Equilibrium, Phase Rule, Single component phase diagrams, Introduction to Multicomponent Multiphase equilibrium, introduction to Classical Mechanics, quantum Mechanics, Canonical Ensemble, Microcanonical Ensemble, Grand Canonical Ensemble, Boltzmann, Fermi-dirac and Bose Einstein Statistics,Fluctuations,Monoatomic and Diatomic Gases, introduction to Classical StatisticalMechanics, phase space, liouville equation, Crystals, Intermolecular forces and potential energy functions, imperfect Monoatomic Gases, Molecular theory of corresponding states, introduction to Molecular Simulations, Mixtures, partial molar properties, Gibbs Duhems equations, fugacity and activity coefficients, Ideal and Non-ideal solutions, Molecular theories of activity coefficients, lattice models, multiphase Multicomponent phase equilibrium, VLE/SLE/LLE/VLLE, Chemical Equilibrium and Combined phase and reactionequilibria.

title: Fluidization Engineering
description: Gross behaviour of fluidized beds, Minimum fluidizing velocity and pressure drops; Voidage, transport disengaging height; Bubbles in dense beds, Davidson Model, stream of bubbles, bubbling bed models, Emulsion phase, Turn-over rate of solids, residence time distribution diffusion model of solids movement, interchange coefficient into and out of wake; Diffusion model for gas flow; two region models, evaluation of interchange coefficients. Mass and heat transfer between fluids and solid from bubbling bed model; Catalytic conversion from bubbling bed model; contacting efficiency; application to successive reactions; Theories and bed-wall heat transfer; comparison of theories; Entrainment and elutrition, application of entrainment model; Residence time distribution and size distribution of solids in fluidized beds, particles of changing size; Circulation rates of solids, flow of high and low bulk density mixtures; Design for catalytic reactors; Design for noncatalytic gas-solid reactors.

title: Pollution Control Systems
description: Sources of water, air and land pollution; Legislation and standards; Analysis of pollution; Design of waste-water and industrial effluent treatment; Specification of Clean technologies and recovery schemes of useful chemicals. Air pollution and its measurement; Design of pollution abatement systems for particulate matter and gaseous constituents; Hazardous waste disposal and treatment; Solid-waste disposal and recovery of useful products through chemical and biological methods.

title: Experimental Methods
description: The lab consists of experiments from the following list, each experiment being coupled with an analytical instrument: Fluid mechanics-Microscopy and Image Analysis; Emulsion liquid membranes-spectrophotometry; Heat effects associated with phase transformation-Differential Scanning Calorimeter / Thermogravimetry; Reactive Distillation-GC/HPLC analysis, Adsorptive separations-High Performance Thin Layer Chromatography; Behavior of monolayers at air-liquid/liquid-liquid interface- Langmuir-Blodgett apparatus; Kinetics of electrochemical reactions- Cyclic voltammetry; Heat of complexation-Microcalorimetry; Catalyst characterization-BET, mercury porosimetry; Rheology of Non-Newtonian Fluids-Rheometry; Characterization of Colloids -Dynamic light scattering and Zeta potential; Material characterization-UTM, film stretching.

timetable.professor: Arindam Sarkar, Chandra Venkataraman, Rajdip Bandyopadhyaya

title: Electrochemical Reaction Engineering
description: 1. Introduction to electrochemistry and examples of electrochemical systems, Electrochemical cells and reactions.2. Non-faradic processes, Electrode-solution interface, and double layer capacitance.3. Factors affecting rates of electrode reactions ( Mass transfer controlled reactions and coupled chemical reactions)4. Thermodynamics of electrochemical systems, cell potentials, liquid junction potentials etc.5. Conductance, transference and mobility6. Kinetics of electrode reactions, Butler-Volmer model and Microscopic theories for charge transfer7. Mass transfer by migration and diffusion 9. Potential step Methods (chronoamperometry)- (Nernstian, Quasireversible and irreversible systems).10. Potential sweep techniques (reversible, irreversible and quasi reversible techniques) and cyclic voltammetry12. Electrochemical Impedance spectroscopy13. Bulk electrolysis14. Mechanisms of electrochemical reactions

title: Multiphase Flow Systems
description: Phase separation and settling behaviour. Description of general two phase systems. Slip and hold-up effect; Horizontal and vertical flow of gas liquid, liquid-liquid, and liquid-solid, gas-solid mixtures; Motion in fluidized beds: Jets and sprays; Chemical reaction in transport systems; Heat and mass transfer effects in suspension flow.

title: Special Topics in Complex Fluids
description: 1. Introduction to Statistical Thermodynamics 2. Intermolecular and Surface forces 3. Thermodynamics and Dynamics of Self Assembly 4. Lipids and Biological membranes 5. Mechanics of Cells 6. Polymeric Suspensions 7. Rheology of Complex Fluids 8. Electrohydrodynamics 9. Computational Techniques

title: Petroleum Refinery Engg. II
description: Thermal Processes-Thermal cracking, Coking, Pyrolysis, Visbreaking; Design of thermal cracking unit, Catalytic Processes-Catalytic Cracking, Catalytic reforming, Application of molecular sieve for refinery processes. Hydrogenation Processes-Hydrogenation, Hydrocracking, Hydro refining, Hydrofinishing,Hydro-desulfurisation.

title: Polymer Reaction Engineering
description: Introduction: Brief introduction to polymer structure; classification of polymers (addition and step growth, thermoplastics and thermosets, linear, branched and network, etc.), molecular weight distributions and averages. Polymerization kinetics and MWD development: Chain growth polymerization-kinetics and kinetic chain length in free radical, anionic and cationic polymerization. Gel effect. Allylic polymerization, (in-situ) casting of polymer products, Molecular weight regulation in chain growth polymerization. Copolymerization kinetics: reactivity ratios and copolymer composition, Walling-Mayo and Skiest equations, MWD prediction, Experimental determination of polymerization kinetics. Step growth polymerization: Types, equal reactivity hypothesis, kinetics and evolution of molecular weight, Deviations from equal reactivity. Reaction engineering of polymerization processes: Bulk, solution, suspension and emulsion polymerization processes. Reactor design for batch, semibatch, CSTR and PFR, influence of mixing on MWD. Heat transfer in polymerization reactors, Additional consideration in the design and operation of commercial polymerization reactors. Special topics in Polymer reaction engineering: Coordination Polymerization, Ziegler-Natta Polymerization, Metallocene Complexes in the Polymerization of Monoolefins. Reactor design issues.

title: Catalysis and Surface Chemistry
description: Review of chemical kinetics, Homogeneous, Acid-Base and Heterogeneous catalysis, Adsorption, Diffusion, Fundamental modeling for catalytic processes, Catalyst preparation and characterization, catalysts in pollution control abatement, Introduction to industrial catalysis, Biocatalysis.

title: Petroleum Refining Engineering I
description: Chemistry of Petroleum hydrocarbons, Composition of petroleum, and petroleum products. Physico-chemical and thermodynamic properties of petroleum products and their characterization. Oil field refining operations-Dehydration, Desalting, Gas separation. Natural gas production and gas sweetening. Refinery operations and vacuum distillation. Sequencing of distillation columns. Distillation column design. Tube still heater design.

title: Polymer Processing
description: Processes, Importance of processing. Viscous non-Newtonian flow: Behavior of polymer melts, One-dimensional isothermal flows, Approximations. Visco-elastic non-Newtonian flow: Phenomena and material functions, Constitutive equations, Rheometry. Non-isothermal aspects: Effect on rhelogical properties. Thermal transport properties, Transient heat conduction, Radiation, Crystallization- morphology and orientation. Mixing: Characterization of mixed state, Dispersive and distributive mixing, Thermodynamics. Extrusion dies: Visco-elastic phenomena, Sheet and film dies. Annular dies. Profile extrusion dies. Multilayer extrusion, Extruders: Extruder types. Hopper design, Plasticating single screw extruder. Postdie processing: Fibre spinning. Film blowing, Moulding and forming- Injection moulding, Thermoforming.

title: Process Modelling and Identification
description: Development of first principle models and linearization, State estimation for linear perturbation models (Luenberger observer), Models for unmeasured disturbances and Kalman filtering, Innovation form of state space model, Extended Kalman filtering , Development of Grey box models (using ANN etc.), Discrete time series models: FIR and ARX models, Development of ARX models by least square estimation, Unmeasured disturbance Modeling: ARMAX, OE, Box-Jenkin?s models, Parameter estimation using prediction error method and instrumental variable method, Distribution of bias and variance errors, Input signal requirements for identification experiments, Control Relevant Identification, MIMO system identification?Issues of directionality, scaling and input signal design. U-D factorization and AUDI algorithms, Recursive approaches to identification and forgetting factor specification, Introduction to nonlinear time series models (Hammerstein / Weiner / NARX) and parameter estimation, Subspace identification techniques and development of state space models, Closed loop identification, Introduction to adaptive control.

title: Modeling and Analysis of Bioprocesses
description: Structured growth models; compartmental models, cybernetic and optimal models, stoichiometric models, immobilized enzyme kinetics, multi-enzyme kinetics. Reactor design, use of structured models, bubble column reactors, fed-batch operation, immobilized cell reactors, immobilized cell reactor/separator, simultaneous enzyme and cell reactors. Metabolic Engineering, Flux analysis, Biochemical Systems Theory (BST), Metabolic Control Analysis (MCA), Genetic models, Protein production kinetics, Analysis of genetic switches.

title: Polymer Dynamics
description: Viscosity of Polymer Melts and Solution: Viscosity of Concentrated Solutions and Melts, Effect of Branching on Viscosity, Elasticity and Viscoelasticity, Maxwell Model for Viscoelasticity,Flow phenomena in polymeric liquids, Brownian Motion, Smoluchowski and Langevin Equation, Autocorrelation and Cross-Correlation functions, Response Function, Fluctuation Dissipation Theorem, Interacting Brownian Particles, Oseen Tensor, microscopic basis of viscoelasticity. Dilute Solutions: Elastic Dumbell Model and bead-rod-spring model for polymer chain, the Rouse and Zimm Models, Viscoelasticity and Birefringence. Semidilute and Concentrated Solutions and melts: Effective Medium Theory, Entanglement Effect, Tube Model and Reptation Model, Network theories, Linear Viscoelasticity, Stress Relaxation, Non-Linear Viscoelasticity, Dynamics of Rigid Rodlike Polymers.

title: Advanced Process Optimization
description: Optimization strategies for Large Scale Plants; Global Optimization Techniques ? Simulated Annealing, Genetic Algorithms; Mixed Integer Linear and Nonlinear Optimization; Applications to Planning and Scheduling; Mixed integer optimization models for supply chain management.

title: Reaction Engineering in Dispersed Phase Systems
description: Classification of multiphase reactors/ heterogeneous reaction systems, Basic theories of mass transfer with chemical reaction, model systems and model reactions, Application to complex and multiple reaction schemes, Mass transport in microheterogeneous systems (emulsions, slurries etc.), Design of selected multiphase reactors.

title: Process Monitoring and Fault Diagnosis
description: Preliminaries: Introduction to probability distributions, Confidence intervals and hypothesis testing, Analysis of variances, wavelets and multiscale analysis. Statistical Quality Control: Introduction to control charts, X and R charts, control charts for individual measurements, CUSUM charts, other SPC problem solving tools, wavelet Methods for Statistical Process Monitoring: Simple linear regression, Multiple linear regression (MLR), Principal Component Analysis (PCA), Projection to Latent Structures (PLS) Performance assessment of control loops for univariate and multivariate cases, Minimum variance benchmarking, LQG benchmarking Introduction to model based fault diagnosis, Fault diagnosis using parameter identification Generalized likelihood ratio (GLR) method, Concepts of analytical redundancy, parity equations and residual generators, design of structured residuals, design of directional residuals. Detection and diagnosis of parametric faults, Neural Networks as Pattern classifiers, NN based classification of process operation into normal and faulty modes, Steady state and Dynamic Data Reconciliation.

title: Rheology of Complex Fluids
description: The course will largely follow Prof. Ronald Larson’s book titled Structure and Rheology of Complex Fluids. We will begin with an introduction to linear and non-linear viscoelasticity, flow-fields, stresses, etc. Complex fluids such as suspensions, polymer solutions, and soft colloidal glasses will be discussed. Phenomenological models and theories will be presented. Some of the measurement techniques including rheometry, microscopy, etc. will be introduced.

title: State Estimation: Theory and Applications
description: This course will deal with theory, tools and techniques for estimation of process states (variables) in the presence of uncertainty. This uncertainty is in the form of uncertainty (noise) in measurements as well as disturbances/noise in the process. The theory covered will deal with estimation for dynamic systems for both linear and nonlinear processes, as well as for gaussian and non-gaussian noise characteristics. Issues related to constraints on process states (constrained estimation), and delayed and multirate measurements will also be dealt with. It is expected that this course will expose the student to not only the well-accepted basics of estimation, but also to recent advances in this area. Throughout the course, the main ideas will be illustrated using matlab-based examples. The following topics will be covered: (i) Linear Optimal (Kalman) Filtering: Least squares formulation, Stochastic formulation, Riccatti equation and solution techniques, Sensitivity and sub-optimal filtering, Smoothing. (ii) Nonlinear Filtering with Gaussian Noise: Extended Kalman filtering, Unscented Kalman filtering. (iii) Particle Filtering: Sequential Monte-Carlo (SMC) methods, Markov processes, SMC based particle filtering, Resampling-MOVE algorithm, Importance sampling, SMC based smoothing, Self organizing state space models, Regularization in particle filtering. (iv) Miscellaneous Topics: Deterministic linear and nonlinear observers, Combined parameter and state estimation, Constrained moving horizon estimators, Incorporation of delayed and multirate measurements.

title: Colloid and Interfacial Engineering
description: Colloidal materials; Brownian motion and Brownian flocculation, Colloidal and interfacial forces, Interfaces, interfacial tension and contact angle, thermodynamics of interfaces, Stability of colloidal dispersions and interfaces, Electrokinetic phenomena and electroviscous flows, Capillary hydrostatics and thin films, Industrial applications.

title: Bioprocess Principles
description: Introduction to biosystems: microorganisms, molecules, genetics, recombinant DNA systems. Principles of enzyme catalysis: enzyme kinetics, enzyme inhibition, enzyme and cell immobilization. Microbial growth and production: stoichiometry, energetics, unstructured and structured models, flux analyses, control analysis. Bioreactors: CSTR, PFR, fed batch, gas-liquid mass transfer, power requirements, control, data acquisition, sterilization, high-cell density cultivation. Separation methods: centrifugation, filtration, ultrafiltration, precipitation, chromatography, and electrophoresis. Other topics: bioinformatics, new classes of molecules, manufacturing, regulatory issues, and economic analyses.

title: Introduction to Computational Biology
description: Flood of data on genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, phenomics, and xxx-omics has led to the emergence of a new inter-disciplinary field where organization, analysis, and processing of this data has to be done efficiently. The biological database are of many different kinds and more and more are emerging with the advent of the technology in the respective field. Although the differnt databases are related to each other, the link is not always clear. The challenge lies in understanding the link between these databases to glean new biological information. Another avenbue is to apply systems science approach to understand biology better. The course introduces students to biological databases, inter-and intra-database relationships, various algorithms, verbal models, quantitative models, etc. The course would cater to both biologist as well as non-biologists.

title: Introduction to Systems Biology
description: Complexity in biological systems; Basic concepts of biological networks; Genetic networks, Transcription networks, Protein networks; Network motifs; Deterministic modeling approaches – kinetics, dynamics; Dynamics of and multiplicity in biological systems – bistability, oscillations, synchronization; Introduction to probability; Stochastic modeling approaches – Introduction, Langevin technique, Master equations, quasi-steady state approximation; Numerical simulations – Gillespie algorithm, Monte Carlo simulations; Fluctuations and robustness; Pattern formation; Traveling waves in biological systems; Quantitative experimentation.

title: Polymer Thermodynamics
description: Polymer Chain Conformations: Random flight model for polymer, The Gaussian Chain, Chain Conformation under an External Field, Excluded Volume Effect and Theta Condition, Rotational Isomeric States, Scaling Concept. Mechanical Behavior of Polymers: Rubber Elasticity, Network Model. Thermodynamics of Polymer Solutions: Dilute, Semidilute and Concentrated Solutions, Osmotic Pressure - MW Measurement, Zimm Diagram - MW Measurement, Viscosity of Dilute Solutions - MW Measurement, Screening Effect, Flory-Huggins Theory, Phase Equilibria in Polymer Solutions, Phase Behaviour of Block Copolymers. Glassy, Crystalline and Molten States of Polymers: Thermodynamics of Melting of Polymers, Kinetics of Crystallization (Avrami Equation), Thermodynamics of Glass Transition, Free Volume theory. Thermodynamic Characterization of Polymers: DSC

title: Sustainable Engineering Principles
description: Part I: Overview and basics (~4 hours)  Basics of sustainability and sustainable development  Brundtland commission report, Millennium Ecosystems Assessment, IPCC report  Evolution from green engineering to sustainable engineering  Motivating examples from chemical as well as other branches of engineering Part II: Sustainability quantification (~15 hours)  Why quantification is important?  Different quantification methods: o Brief review of well established economic indicators o Footprint analysis (carbon footprint, water footprint) o Life cycle inventory and assessment o Ecological/environmental carrying capacity o Information theory based indicators for dynamic systems o Resource use intensity o Exergy, emergy etc. o Social indicators Part III: Design of sustainable systems (~15 hours)  Importance of incorporating sustainability in design  Examples of sustainable designs  Design methods for sustainability o Moving from quantification to decision making (design) o LCA o System dynamics models o Other methods  1-2 Hands-on activities using freely available tools such as the OpenLCA (http://www.openlca.org/) Part IV: Case studies: Sustainable design in chemical engineering and other allied fields (~ 6 hours)  Examples of performing sustainable design from different fields such as a energy, water, material use, solvent selection, ionic liquids  Guest lectures by domain experts (e.g. faculty from CTARA, HSS or from outside of IITB), and demonstration using educational modules (e.g. Center for Sustainable Engineering, http://www.csengin.org/csengine/)

title: Product Research & Development
description: The course will be an introduction to the methodology, skills, and "competencies" required for target/objective oriented R&D in creation of Chemical Technology embedded "Products". The "Products" under consideration could be consumer goods, foods, specialty chemicals, equipment or devices. The course will include case studies demonstrating the process of Product R&D, as well as application of rigorous Scientific and Chemical Engineering principles, to solve problems posed by real life products. Apart from class room lectures, the students will have to carry out short term assignments/projects. The course would be of benefit to those interested in pursuing careers in R&D or Product Innovation. (A few interested 2nd, 3rd,4th Year B.Tech students will be permitted to opt for the course, although it is open, primarily to 5th year Dual Degree & PG students.)

title: Multiscale Simulations
description: · Intermolecular forces (van der Waals, Electrostatic) · Force-fields (Atomistic and molecular system) · Potential models (Lennard-Jones, Coulombic, Bonded Potential) · Structural properties (Radial distribution function, Potential energy, Pressure, structure factor, s(k)) · Dynamic Properties (Diffusivity from MSD and/or VACF, Thermal conductivity) · Periodic Boundary Conditions (PBC) and minimum image convention · Bond constraints for molecular systems · Molecular Dynamics Methodology (through its implementation in NVE, NVT and NPT ensemble) · Monte Carlo Method (Importance Sampling, Partition function and probability density for NVT, NPT, uVT ensembles) · Introduction to coarse-graining techniques Introduction to DFT/ab-initio methodology

title: Applied Multivariate Statistics in Chemical Engineering
description: x

title: Turbulent Multiphase Flow
description: Single phase turbulence Introduction to stability and transition. Vorticity dynamics. Origin and characteristics of turbulence. Spatial and temporal correlations. Time and length scales in turbulence. Momentum transport and concept of Reynolds stress. Kinetic Energy of turbulence. Spectral dynamics and energy cascading. Multiphase dynamics Properties of dispersed phase flows. Dilute versus dense phase flows. Interphase coupling between particles/droplets-fluid for mass, momentum, and energy transport. Particle-particle interactions. Phenomena of particle clustering, turbulence attenuation and augmentation, turbophoresis. Modeling and Computational methods Lagrangian-Eulerian and Eulerian-Eulerian approach to model multiphase turbulent flow. Introduction to direct numerical simulation using spectral method. Hard sphere and discrete element model (DEM) to address dispersed phase interaction.

title: Theoretical Ecology
description: Course Content (List of the topics/sub-topics to be covered in the lectures/practicals/assignments): Growth models for populations and communities (exponential and geometric growth; spatial, temporal, and individual variation models; Demographic relationships; Population Regulation). Stochasticity in model formulations. Ecological Metabolic Organisation (Dynamic Energy Budget Theory; Metabolic interactions among individuals; Evolution of metabolic systems in a community). Evolutionary Ecology; Evolutionary dynamics of biological organisation in an ecosystem. Epidemiology. Ecological Networks.

title: Analytical and Numerical Techniques in Hydrodynamic Stability
description: • (Mathematical preliminaries) Local classification of linear ode`s with variable coefficients: ordinary, regular and irregular singular points, Taylor and Frobenius series expansions, regular and singular Sturm-Liouville eigenvalue problems, contour integration on the complex plane, Cauchy principle value of integrals, asymptotic evaluation of inverse Laplace integrals, introduction to Airy and Gauss hypergeometric functions, Ricati transformation.  • (General ideas in stability) Rayleigh and Orr-Sommerfeld equations, necessary conditions for inviscid instability (Rayleigh and Fjortof theorems), Tollmien`s  series solutions to the Rayleigh equation, singularity of the Rayleigh equation and the critical layer, physical implication of the logarithmic singularity at the critical layer, applications of singular perturbation techniques to the Orr-Sommerfeld equation, Lin`s phase change rule, Reynolds stress at the critical layer and various inviscid instabilities, Tollmien-Schlichting instability, normal mode analysis versus initial value problem, completeness of eigenfunctions, regular and singular eigenfunctions, discrete and continuous spectra, negative energy waves, over-reflection and instability. • Stability of thin film flow on an inclined plane, Reynolds, Weber and Kapitza numbers, long and short wave instabilities, Kuramoto-Sivashinsky equation, KdV equation, thin film equations with Van der Waals force, rupture of ultra-thin films, thermocapillary instabilities e.g. Rayleigh-Benard, Benard-Marangoni instability, evaporative instabilities, Mullins-Sekerka’s instability in crystal growth. • Parallel shear flows with density stratification, Taylor-Goldstein equation and Richardson number Ri > 1/4 criteria, Holmboe instability, Taylor-Caulfield instability, Linearised internal gravity (IG) waves with and without shear, parametric subharmonic instabilities, instabilities due to interactions of vorticity and density waves in multilayered flows, instabilities due to viscosity stratification. • (Numerical Techniques in stabiltiy) Compound matrix methods, numerical solution to the Rayleigh equation, Chebyshev polynomials, numerical solution to the Orr-Sommerfeld equation, QR eigenvalue algorithm, spectral methods and spectral accuracy. • (Additional topics in vortex instabilities) Stability of a vortex sheet, stability of a polygonal array of vortices, stability of the Karman vortex street, dynamics of vortex filaments, local induction approximation, Kelvin waves on a vortex filament, oscillations of a vortex column, long wave cooperative instabilities etc.

title: Modeling and Simulation
description: Modelling of Staged Processes - Equilibrium and Rate Based Approaches; Modelling of Differential Contactors - Lumped/Distributed Parameter Models of Packed Beds: Adsorption Columns, Packed Bed Reactors; Model Reduction through Orthogonal Collocation; Modelling of Reactive Separation Processes; Solution Strategies for Large Sparse Nonlinear Algebraic Equations, Stiff and Non-stiff Differential Equations, Differential Algebraic Equations (DAEs), and Partial Differential Equations (PDEs).

title: Modelling Stochastic and Turbulent Transport
description: • Motivation with examples of stochastic transport phenomena: micro-swimmers, surface growth, polymers, turbulence. • Historical introduction to Brownian motion and diffusion: Einstein’s and Langevin’s complementary approaches—probability distributions vs noisy trajectories. • Revision of probability concepts and introduction to stochastic Markov processes. • Derivation of the Fokker-Plank (FP) equation via a probability balance (i.e. the Chapman Kolmogorov equation); forward and backward FP equations. • Stochastic differential equations (SDEs): revision of integration theory (Riemann vs Lebesgue integrals); Ito and Stratonovich stochastic integrals—mathematical convenience vs physical relevance; SDE in the Ito interpretation; Ito’s formula; converting from Stratonovich to Ito; difference between additive and multiplicative noise. • Derivation of the Fokker-Plank equation from a stochastic differential equation; connections between the two. • Numerical methods for solving Fokker-Plank and Stochastic differential equations; comparison of both approaches for Brownian motion; application to active Brownian particles (microswimmers); brief introduction to collective behavior of interacting active particles. • Growth or propagation of rough interfaces: invalidity of a surface integral; scaling and fractal concepts; Kardar-Parisi-Zhang (KPZ) equation; renormalization group; spatially-correlated noise; applications in fluid flow through porous media/paper wetting/growth of bacterial colonies/burning fronts or flames; Burgers turbulence (Burgulence). • Polymer solutions – an archetypical complex fluid: examples of viscoelastic phenomena in laminar and turbulent flows (drag reduction and elastic turbulence); elastic dumbbell as a coarse-grained polymer; polymer stretching in linear flows. • Turbulence: whence the apparent randomness (brief introduction to chaos); scalar mixing in turbulence—modelling turbulence as a random (Kraichnan) flow and the foundations of the concept of eddy diffusivity; pattern formation in turbulent flows; stretching of polymers in turbulence; epilog—how random is turbulence?

title: Techno-Commerical Aspects of Fine Chemicals
description: Overview of F&S Chemicals industry sectors: AGROCHE MICALS, FLAVORS AND FRAGRANCES, DYES AND PIGMENTS, SURFACTANTS, POLYMER ADDITIVES, PERSONAL CARE INGREDIENTS, CONSTRUCTION CHEMICALS, WATER CHEMICAL S, TEXTILE CHEMICALS Manufacturing Practices: Introduction, Catalysis, S electivity Engineering, Separation processes, Process Development. Commercial Aspects: Introduction to the market (wit h special focus on Indian Industry), market size and growth, Key success factors, trends , Indian Companies in this sector, Future outlook.

title: Introduction to Chemical Engineering Applications in Industry
description: 1 Pharma Business: Overview 2 Pharma: The Chemical Engineers` Disney World 3 Oral Solid Dosage Forms: Concepts in Powder Flow 4 Oral Solid Dosage Forms: Powder Blending-1 5 Oral Solid Dosage Forms: Powder Blending-2 6 Oral Solid Dosage Forms: Granulation/ High Shear Granulation 7 Oral Solid Dosage Forms: Granulation/Fluid Bed Granulation-1 8 Oral Solid Dosage Forms: Granulation/Fluid Bed Granulation-2 9 Oral Solid Dosage Forms: Granulation/Twin Screw Granulation 10 Oral Solid Dosage Forms: Granulation/Roller Compaction 11 Oral Solid Dosage Forms: Tablet Compression 12 Oral Solid Dosage Forms: Tablet Coating 13 Mathematical Modeling of Solid Flows: Discrete Element Modeling (DEM) 14 Mathematical Modeling of Solid Flows: Industrial Case Studies Leveraging DEM 15 Mathematical Modeling of Solid Flows: Practical sessions using of DEM tools 16 Fluid (/Solid) Mixing in Processes: Issues and Challenges 17 Fluid(/Solid) Mixing in Processes: Basics 18 Mixing and Phase Separations (Anti-Solvent Crystallization, etc) 19 Mathematical Modeling of Fluid Flows: Industrial Case Studies leveraing CFD 20 Effectively Leveraging the Power of Simulations for Industrial Problem Solving 21 Emusification: Rotor-Stator Mixers 22 Dispersing of Particles in Liquid: Rotor-Stator Mixers 23 Manufacturing of Nano-Emulsion: High Pressure Homogenization 24 Manufacturing of Nano-Emulsion: High Pressure Homogenization (Modeling Aspects) 25 Impact of Surfactants and Flow: Emusification, Particle Dispersions 26 Drying: Various Types and Process Challenges 27 Drying: Agitated Netzsch Filtration and Drying- Basics and Bringing Predictablity 28 Drying: Freeze drying/ Lyophilization-Basics-1 29 Drying: Freeze drying/ Lyophilization-Bringing Predictability-2 30 Drying: Freeze drying/ Lyophilization-Practical Problem Solving-3 31 Terminal Sterilization 32 Terminal Sterilization (Bringing Predictability) 33 Drug Release Kinetics - Overview of Methods and Challenges-1 34 Drug Release Kinetics - Bringing Predictability, Via Case Studies-2

title: Computer Aided Design & Engineering
description: Computer Aided Design and Engineering Tools; Process Simulators ? Steady State Simulators, Dynamic Simulators; Control System Design Environments; Process and Energy Integration software Packages; Heat Exchanger Network synthesis software; Computational Fluid Dynamics and related software; Problem solving in the areas of Process Synthesis, Process Simulation, Design & Optimization, Process Control, and CFD Modeling applications; Expert Systems and other Artificial Intelligence applications.

title: Advanced Process Synthesis
description: Development of Chemical Process Systems; Process Integration, Philosophy of Targeting, Thermodynamic Approach, Mathematical Programming Approach; Heat Exchanger Networks and Energy Optimization; Utility and Power Systems; Total Site Analysis; Separation Systems, Distillation Column Sequencing and Integration; Mass Exchange Networks; Networks for Water and Hydrogen Management; Reaction Path Synthesis, Reactor Networks, Reactor-Separator-Recycle Systems: Grassroots and Retrofit Designs, Multiple Base Case Designs; Flexibility Considerations; Industrial Applications and Case Studies.

title: Advanced Process Control
description: Review of Single Input Single Output (SISO) Control; Model Based Control; Multivariable control strategies; Internal Model Control Preliminaries and Model Predictive Control, Model forms for Model Predictive Control, Model forms for Model Predictive Control; Parametric and Non-parametric Models, State space and Transfer Function Representations and their inter relationships; Control Relevant Proces identification; Choice of Input Signals and Model Forms; Parameter Estimation using batch and Recursive Least Squares; Model Validations using Correlation Concepts; Identification of Non-parametric Representations; Model Predictive Control; Analysis of Dynamic Matrix Control (DMC) and Generalized Predictive Control (GPC) Schemes, Controller Tunning and Robustness Issues; Extensions to Constrained and Multivariable Cases.

title: Artificial Intelligence in Process Engineering
description: Intorduction-History and Relation of Artificial Intelligence (AI) to Process Engineering; Knowledge Representation I- Predicate calculus and Semantic Networks; Search-forward/Backward. depth/breadth/best - first search; Production Systems; History; Components; Knowledge Representation II - Frames, Objects; Inexact Reasoning - Introduction, Blackboard Architecture; Expert Systems - Applications to Industry' Programming Languages; Expert System Shells; Neural Nets - Introduction and Application to Process Engineering.

title: Digital Control
description: State space models, discretisation, ZOH equivalent, basic system, concepts, Z-transforms, transfer function, internal stability, frequecncy domain analysis, Fourier series and transforms. Identification, Traditional control techniques. State space techniques. Model predictive control, and adaptive control.

title: Computational Methods in Chemical Engineering
description: Errors and error control; Interpolation and optimization; Linear algebraic equations; Matrix inversion and matrix eigenvalues estimation; Solution of non-linear algebraic equations; Newton Raphson technique and homotopy; Numerical integration; Ordinary differential equations; Runge Kutta Techniques; Predictor corrector methods; solution of partial differential equations; implicit and explicit techniques; finite volume and finite element methods; Differential algebraic equations; Introduction to equation solving software like MATLAB, DSSL, LSODE etc.

title: Lecture Series
description: Course Content: The students are required to attend lectures on a wide variety of topics relevant to Chemical Engineering research and industry, presented by persons of high standing in industry and other academic institutions, faculty from the chemical Engineering and the other Departments /Centres /Schools at IIT-B and PhD Students at advanced stages in their Research. Assessment of the performance would be based on attendance alone.

title: Lecture Series
description: Course Content: The students are required to attend lectures on a wide variety of topics relevant to Chemical Engineering research and industry, presented by persons of high standing in industry and other academic institutions, faculty from the chemical Engineering and the other Departments /Centres /Schools at IIT-B and PhD Students at advanced stages in their Research. Assessment of the performance would be based on attendance alone.

title: Molecular Principles in Chemical Engineering
description: Molecular Structure of Matter – states of matter, solids and bonding, amorphous solids, density and distribution functions of liquids, non-ideal gases, kinetic theory of gases, Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution, molecular origins of transport. Solution Thermodynamics – review of the laws of thermodynamics and the Boltzmann equation for entropy, chemical potential and its relation to equilibrium, ideal and non-ideal solutions, colligative properties, Debye Huckel Theory. Theories of Chemical Reactions/Chemical Kinetics – mechanisms and rate laws, transition state theory, collision theory, activated complex theory, transport properties, catalysis, reactions in fluids, reactions on solid surfaces.

title: Processing with Supercritical Fluids
description: -Mukhopadhyay, M., Natural Extracts Using Supercritical Carbon Dioxide, CRC Press LLC, Florida, USA, 2000. -McHugh M.A., and Krukonis, V.J., Supercritical Fluid Extraction: Principles and Practise, Butterworth-Heinemann, Stoneham, MA, USA, 1994. -York P., Kompella, U.B., and Shekonov, B.Y., Supercritical Fluid Technology for Drug Product Development, Marcel Dekker Inc., New Yark,USA,2004. -Jessop, P.G. and Leitner, W., Chemical Systhesis Using Supercritical Fluids, Wiley-VCH, Weinheim, Germany, 1999.

title: Process Data Analysis and Design of Experiments
description: This course will deal with statistical analysis of engineering process data and the design of experiments. Specifically, this course will be concerned with building empirical models (linear as well as nonlinear models) from given data, analyzing the developed models for adequacy, and refining the model if required. Data analysis methods based on covariance structures of the variables will also be discussed. Further, generation of data in an â€œoptimalâ€ sense by using designed experiments will also be covered. Techniques for finding new operating regions for improved process performance will be discussed. Throughout the course, the main ideas will be illustrated by examples based on experimental or industrial data. The following topics will be covered: Basic Statistics: Confidence intervals; Hypothesis testing; Estimators and their properties Linear and Nonlinear Regression: Maximum-Likelihood estimation; Confidence intervals in linear regression; Hidden extrapolation and multicollinearity in multiple linear regression; Statistical inference in nonlinear regression; Bootstrapping in nonlinear regression Model Adequacy Analysis: Residual analysis: Lack of fit test; Analysis of variance Other Topics in Regression: Transformation and weighting to correct model inadequacies; Variable selection and model building; Generalized linear models; Non-parametric regression techniques Classification and regression trees. Methods based on covariance structures Principal components analysis; Canonical correlation analysis Design of Experiments: Design and analysis of single factor experiments; Experiments to compare k treatment means; 2 level factorial designs for design of experiments with several factors; Analysis of variance in factorial designs; Calculation of effects and relation to regression Blocking 2 level fractional factorial designs with several factors Generators, defining relations, aliasing, design resolution, projection, and fold-over designs in fractional factorial designs Response Surface Methodologies Direction of steepest ascent; Rotatable second order designs

title: Introduction to Network Analysis
description: Introduction to network and Motivation.Network as a model and a graph. Elements of basic graph theory.Simulation and analyses. Networks metrics and operation. Analyses iof large netwroks.Sub-networks approach.Network decomposition -Motif finding Modularity.Large Network approach: Boolean dynamics modeling.Case studies- 1. Process networks; Sensor network, Reaction Networks, Water distribution networks.2, biological networks: Cellular networks, Disease Networks.Tools Matlab.

title: Nonlinear Systems Analysis
description: This course will cover the following topics: a) Some common distinguishing properties of nonlinear systems b) Bifurcation and limit cycles c) Perturbation methods d) Chaos e) Lyapunov stability

title: Classical Studies in Molecular Biology and Genetics
description: • Early studies Mendelian genetics in humans, • One gene, one enzyme hypothesis, • DNA as the transforming principle, • Mutations and fluctuation test, • Structure of DNA and its implications, • Topology and topography of a gene, • Discovery of the genetic code, • Recombination in bacteria, • Principles of gene regulation, • Role of noise in dictating cellular physiology.

title: Modelling Chemical and Biiological Patterns
description: Temporal patterns in chemical and biological systems – Introduction to temporally oscillating chemical and biological systems. Examples from chemistry such as CIDMA, Brusselator, B-Z reactions. Examples from biology such as circadian rhythms. Experimental observations and measurements. Modeling and some analytical solutions to ODEs. Spatio-temporal pattern formation and analytical solutions – Introduction to spatial pattern formation in chemical and biological systems. Reaction-diffusion systems. BZ- reactions, Turing patterns. Spatial patterns in reaction – diffusion-advection systems. Stationary and travelling waves. Modeling of spatio-temporal patterns. Some analytical solutions to PDEs. Computer simulation of spatio-temporal dynamics – Numerical solutions to PDEs. Simulating the evolution of spatio-temporal patterns in chemical and biological systems.

title: Evolutionary Dynamics
description: Introduction to Evolution; Fitness Landscape and Sequence Spaces; Evolutionary Game Theory (fitness dependent on frequency);Origin of Cooperation (genes to genomes; single-cell to multicellular organisms); Deterministic and Stochastic Description of Finite Populations; Evolutionary Graph Theory; Infinite Populations; Modeling Ecological Dynamics; &Examples: Evolution of Cancer, HIV, Virulence, and Language.

title: Introduction to Cell Mechanics
description: Review of cell biology in general; shape, size and function. Introduction to intracellular structures and their functions: membrane, cytoplasm, cytoskeletal elements and nucleus. Introduction to Cell mechanics and its importance in biology under normal and pathological conditions. Review of rheology and surface tension. Cell membrane: description of bi-layer structure, membrane tension, Laplace’s equation, micro-aspiration technique and related calculations. Cytoskeletal elements: Structure of actin, microtubule, molecular motors, related mechanisms. Review of stress-strain and solid mechanics. Cellular rheology and associated experimental methods; AFM, optical tweezers. Effect of cell mechanics on cellular functions. Cell types covered; stem cells, RBC and cardiomyocytes. Cell response to flow and strain. Cell migration, single and collective. Introduction to micro-fabrication and lithography. Micro-contact printing. Methods to measure cellular contractility; wrinkle on substrate, traction force microscopy, micro pillars and associated calculation. Mechanism of mechanotransduction; ion-channel, stretchy proteins, molecular pathways. Tensegrity model and nuclear deformation. Diseases caused by problems in cellular/nuclear mechanics.

title: Introduction to Bio-membranes
description: Introduction: Introduction to a cell and organelles, role of cell membrane, chemistry and structure of lipids and proteins, fundamentals of self-assembly and applications to membranes and vesicles, phase transition in membranes, Interaction of proteins with membranes, multicomponent membranes, concept of line tension, concept of a raft, membrane permeation Experimental: Synthesis of vesicles, classification based on size, hydration, extrusion, sonication, electroformation, experimental techniques in bio-membranes and vesicles,  ESR, Neutron scattering to measure bilayer thickness, FRAP and other fluorescence based assays for diffusion / phase separation due to temperature and tension variation Membrane Elasticity: Elasticity in membranes: Hookes law, Skalak’s law, etc, gaussian, mean and spontaneous curvature, bending moduli, mechanical properties of a monolayer and a bilayer membrane, Helfrich’s free energy, shape equation for membranes, thermal fluctuations in membranes and application to micropipette method, Simulation: Overview of bio-membrane simulations, all atom simulations, coarse grained simulations, MD,MC and DPD meethods Response to External forces:  Pearling instabilities, budding, fission, fusion and adhesion in vesicles, vesicles in shear flow, active particles in vesicles, applications of liposomes, diffusion in membranes, electroporation and electrofusion in vesicles, electrophysiology, patch clamp

title: Introduction to Interfacial Waves
description: Introduction to oscillations: Oscillations in discrete systems, coupled system of discrete oscillators, normal-modes, Oscillations in continuous media.Interfacial Flows: Boundary conditions at an interface, Non-dimensionalisation and scaling, Derivation of equations and boundary conditions from a variational principle.Sound Waves: Acoustic waves, Speed of sound, Acoustic energy and intensity, Simple Source, Acoustic Dipole, Compact Source Regions in General, Ripple-tank simulations Introduction to surface-gravity waves: Surface-gravity waves on water of finite and uniform depth, Dispersion relations, Mixed nature of surface-gravity waves, Deep and shallow-water limits, frequency dispersion, phase-speed and group-velocity, group-velocity for linear dispersive systems, Physical interpretation of group-velocity, Mass-transport in gravity-waves, Stoke302222s drift.Long-waves: Linear and Non-Linear Shallow-water Equations, Analogy between shallow-water theory and compressible flows, hydrostatic and non-hydrostatic effects of pressure, shape of a propagating wave-form in shallow-water theory, non-linear steepening of waves, formation of bores and cnoidal waves.The Initial-Value Problem- Inviscid, irrotational Cauchy-Poisson problem in planar and cylindrical geometry. Kelvin-wave pattern - Wave patterns made by obstacles in a steady stream, Kelvin302222s wake in deep-water, Comparison with a non-dispersive system viz. Mach cone.Stoke`s Expansion - Nonlinear gravity waves of permanent form, Amplitude dispersion.Capillary Waves - Dispersion relation, Anomalous dispersion of capillary waves, non-linear capillary waves of permanent form: Crapper302222s solution, capillary-gravity waves.Additional Topics - Boussinesque and Korteweg-de Vries equation, Historical perspective of the soliton, Fermi-Pasta-Ulam recurrence, Viscous damping of gravity waves, viscous dispersion relations, Linear and non-linear Rayleigh-Taylor and Kelvin Helmholtz instabilities.

title: Technology Design and Development Laboratory
description: The basic components of the course are:1. Lectures/Tutorials: Fundamentals of need, innovation, technology, design, development and deployment - to be covered in about 8 lectures (4 in Jan and 4 in Feb) in the first two months semester. 2. Laboratory: Manufacturing machine/equipment training, hands on practice on the machines - to be covered in 8 lab sessions (4 in Jan and 4 in Feb). Students, who seek to practice further, will have access to extra practice sessions in the first two months of the semester. The students will get trained to use the fabrication machines and develop basic skills so that they should be adequately abled. 3. Mini project: identified by a group of students (likely 3) based on need assessment and on arriving at a clearly defined problem statement. Project work is expected to include definition of the problem - scope of work - a plan to meet problem objective(s) - production of a working unit - testing, demonstration and assessment of its performance - preparation and presentation of a report and a business plan. The project has to be completed in two months, preferably using resources available in the Chemical Engineering workshop, the Tata Centre Lab, or other labs on campus. A letter from Tata Centre extending the facilities has been provided. Students are expected to complete projects on their own. In exceptional cases where facilities or prototyping skills are not available on campus, project can be outsourced on consultation with the course instructors.

title: Introduction to Genomics
description: Genome structure, Overview of traditional and next-generation sequencing technologies, assembly algorithms, analysis of de-novo sequences, RNA-sequencing and transcriptomics, Computational methods for genome analysis including annotation, expression analysis, network inference and data mining. Comparative genomics, Application of genomics in medicine, biotechnology, evolution, metagenomics. Genome editing.

title: Communication Skills -II
description: 1.Demonstration and evaluation of Scientific Reports, Note Taking Techniques 2.Discussion and Debates after Listening, Podcasts and Webcasts 3.Two pass approach to reading papers, Summary writing, literature survey and organization, Software Aids 4.Report Writing, Document Processing Software 5.Ethics and Plagiarism: practice and discussion. 6.Writing and Talking about workplace relationships, Gender Issues, Stereotypes, Biases, Labeling 7.Discussion on Contextual Viewpoint, Non-verbal Communication, Analysis of Mannerisms during interactions and presentations. 8.Evaluation of Presentations, Short and Long Talks, Timing, Visual Aids, Presentation Software

title: Communication Skills
description: Context of communication: Recognizing our capability androles as professionalsScientific Method: Question and answer aspects oftechnical communication; Scientific Methodology and itsrelationship to technical communication;Surveying literature: Categories; reading and organizingscientific literature; search engines and tools.Listening and Note taking: 5-R method and mind-mapping.Technical writing: Report organization; Journal selection;Introduction, conclusion, and abstract writing.Speaking & Presentation skills: Organization ofpresentation slides (number, content, and formatting); Oralpresentations; Audience/context dependent practices; Nonverbal aspects: body language, eye-contact, personalappearance, facing large audience.Elevator pitch: Pitches for technical audience and policymakersWorkplace communication: Sensitivity towards genderand diversity; Email communication and netiquettes.Ethics in academic communication: Intellectual Property,copyrights and plagiarism; Authorship; Data ethics; Biasesand balanced criticism of literature;Suggested additional topics relevant to disciplines: Datarepresentation, Group discussion and interviews; accessiblescientific writing, report writing using LaTeX, Proofreading, etc

title: Earth`s Climate: Past, Present and Future
Advanced computational methods for turbulent incompressible flows
description: The course is designed to teach computational techniques for incompressible turbulent reacting flow, specifically direct numerical simulation (DNS) and large-eddy simulation (LES). These techniques will be discussed in the context of atmospheric and ocean modelling, although they are more widely applicable. These high-resolution methods require parallellisation of the flow solvers, and students will learn the principles of parallel computing, and gain hands-on high- performance computing (HPC) experience in the course project. Key topics: • The equations of fluid dynamics and thermodynamics; conservation of mass, momentum, energy; nondimensional parameters. Forms of the governing equations: compressible, anelastic, Boussinesq (incompressible), shallow-water. • The scales of fluid flow (e.g. in the atmosphere: micro-, meso-, synoptic, planetary scale); suitable forms of governing equations; rotating and stratified flows. • Basics of atmospheric thermodynamics; water vapour and its transformations; dry and moist lapse rates; dry and moist convection. • Turbulence: Kolmogorov (1941) theory; length scales of turbulent flow; resolved and unresolved scales; modelling and closure. History of atmospheric fluid dynamics and turbulence theory. • Numerical methods for the Navier-Stokes equations: discretisation in space: (pseudo- )spectral, finite-difference / finite-volume; collocated and staggered grids; discretisation in time: implicit and explicit time-stepping; error analysis of numerical methods: stability, dispersion, dissipation. • Hierarchy of numerical methods: Direct numerical simulation (DNS), large-eddy simulation (LES), Reynolds averaged Navier-Stokes simulation (RANS); domains of application. • Parallelisation: shared / distributed memory (OpenMP / MPI), communication overheads, weak and strong scaling, acceleration of solvers. • Course project: students will reproduce a peer-reviewed journal article based on DNS / LES. Students will use a suitable open-source numerical solver, or write their own, and deploy these solvers on the IDPCS cluster. Special topics required for specific papers will be covered.

title: Atmospheric Thermodynamics
description: 1. Review of basic concepts: Pressure, Temperature, Density, State variables and state functions, Composition of atmosphere, Ideal gas laws, Equation of State, Mixture of ideal gases 2. First and Second Law of Thermodynamics: Work, Internal energy, Heat, First law, Enthalpy, Adiabatic processes, Water vapor in air, The Carnot cycle, Entropy, Second law, Clausius-Clapeyron relationship, Global energy and entropy balances (all in context of climate sciences) 3. Thermodynamic processes in the atmosphere: Dry and moist processes, Isobaric cooling, Dew and Frost points, Horizontal mixing, Vertical mixing, Adiabatic expansion, Adiabatic ascent and air saturation, Pseudoadiabatic process 4. Vertical Stability of the atmosphere: Parcel Method, Stability Criteria, Lapse Rates, Oscillations in a stable layer, Conditional stability, Convective Available Potential Energy, Processes inducing changes in stability 5. Thermodynamics & Weather Systems: Formation of fronts, Surface weather, Deep convection, Convective storms, Tropical cyclones- Structure, genesis and life cycle, Convection in climate models, Cloud processes 6. Thermodynamics & climate feedbacks: Earth’s energy budget, Radiative forcing, Climate feedbacks, Temperature feedback, Water vapor feedback, Cloud feedback, Albedo feedback, Climate sensitivity

title: Monsoons and Tropical Climate
description: Introduction: Defining the tropics, Role of the tropics in energy balance, Role of tropics in momentum balance, Tropical air-masses and Climate • Important features of tropical climate: Brief history of tropical meteorology, large-scale tropical circulation features, Brief introduction to phenomenon of Inter-tropical convergence zones (ITCZ), El-Nino Southern Oscillation (ENSO), Madden-Julian Oscillation (MJO), Indian Ocean Dipole (IOD), Tropical Cyclones, Arid and Desert Climates • Concept of global monsoons: The coupled monsoon system, Regional monsoons, Phenomenology of Indian monsoon, Early descriptions of monsoon, Land-sea breeze circulations, Monsoon as the seasonal migration of the tropical convergence zone (TCZ) • Monsoon Variability: Some aspects of synoptic, intra-seasonal, inter-annual and inter-decadal variability of Indian monsoon and current state of understanding • Tropical Clouds: Basics of convection, organisation and structure of tropical convection, Latent heating profiles during monsoon, Cloud clusters, Convective momentum transport, Morphology of convective storms • Theoretical aspects of Monsoon dynamics: Energetics of monsoons, Quasi-equilibrium theory of monsoons, Monsoons as angular momentum conserving circulations, knowledge gaps in current understanding • Projected changes in Monsoons: Summer monsoon – Projections for South Asian monsoon, east Asian monsoon, Australian Monsoon, monsoon onset and ENSO-Monsoon relationship, Winter Monsoons in India • The Asian monsoon – Agriculture and Economy

title: Atmospheric Radiative Transfer and Climate
description: • Introduction & elementary concepts: : Why study atmospheric radiation? relevance to climate, climate change and remote sensing, Radiation, Properties of radiation, Nature of Electromagnetic (EM) radiation, EM spectrum, Solar & terrestrial radiation, Global heat engine, Earth’s energy budget, Faint Young Sun, Snowball Earth, Hysteresis. • Radiation from blackbody: Definition and properties of blackbody, Emissive characteristics of blackbody, Planck’s function, Wein’s displacement law, Stefan-Boltzmann law, Rayleigh-Jean approximation. Radiative properties of real surfaces: Emissivity, Absorptivity, Reflectivity, Graybody approximation, Graybody emissivity, Kirchhoff’s law, brightness temperature. • Atmospheric scattering, absorption & emission: Extinction,Scattering and Absorption Coefficients, Plane Parallel Approximation, Schwarzschild`s Equation, Radiative Transfer in a Plane Parallel Atmosphere, The Spectrum of Atmospheric Emission. • Radiation & atmospheric gases: Basis for Absorption/Emission, Absorption/Emission Lines, absorption. • Applications of thermal emission: Radiative equilibrium, Top-of-atmosphere radiation balance, radiative cooling, Looking from space, Simple radiation models of the atmosphere. • Aerosols, cloud & radiation: Radiation interaction with aerosols and clouds, radiative convective equilibrium, net radiative effect of clouds, role of clouds in energy balance. • Energy balance & hydrological cycle: The Surface Energy Budget, Storage of Heat in the Surface, Radiative Heating of the Surface, Variation of Energy Balance Components with Latitude, Diurnal Variation of the Surface Energy Balance, Relation to hydrological cycle.

title: Numerical Methods for Climate Science
description: Key topics: • Crash course on Vector calculus • Numerical solution of algebraic and transcendental equations, iterative algorithms, convergence. Optimisation and root finding algorithms for linear and nonlinear systems of equations. • Numerical differentiation and integration: methods of integration and differentiation, order and accuracy, error estimation. • Ordinary differential equations: order, linearity. Numerical solution: discretisation, accuracy and stability. Initial and boundary value problems; eigenvalue problems. Stiff equations. • Partial differential equations. properties of partial differential equations: order, linearity / nonlinearity, well-posedness, ellipticity / hyperbolicity. Initial and boundary value problems. • Numerical methods for partial differential equations: discretisation in space: (pseudo-)spectral, finite-difference / finite-volume; collocated and staggered grids; discretisation in time: implicit and explicit time-stepping. Error analysis of numerical methods: stability, dispersion, dissipation. • Choosing numerical methods based on the properties of the governing equations: central and one-sided discretisations; high- and low- order discretisations; ENO/WENO schemes. • Fluid dynamics. Lagrangian and Eulerian descriptions of fluid dynamics, Reynolds transport theorem, constitutive relations, Reynolds transport theorem, conservation of mass, momentum, energy; nondimensional parameters. Advanced topics: • Approximations of the fully compressible governing equations: anelastic, Boussinesq / incompressible, shallow-water. The scales of fluid flow (e.g. in the atmosphere: micro-, meso-, synoptic, planetary scale). • Hierarchy of numerical methods: Direct numerical simulation (DNS), large-eddy simulation (LES), Reynolds averaged Navier-Stokes simulation (RANS); domains of application.

title: General Circulation of the Atmosphere and Oceans
description: The Atmospheric general circulation: The Observed General Circulation- The Time-Averaged General Circulation, The Zonally Averaged General Circulation, Eddies and Transients, The High Frequency Extratropical Transients, The Low Frequency Extratropical Transients, Planetary-Scale Rossby Waves, Blocking, Annual Mean Circulation of the Tropics, What Determines the Annual Mean Rain Rate Climatology? Day-to-Day Variability of the Tropical Circulation (Equatorial waves, monsoon disturbances), Tropical Cyclones Ocean general circulation: Open-ocean currents, Forcing Mechanisms, Wind-Driven Currents, Ekman Spiral, Thermally driven ocean circulation, Subtropical Gyres, western-boundary currents, Eddies, Eastern Boundary Currents, Subpolar Gyres, Monsoon-Driven Currents in the Northern Indian Ocean

title: Atmosphere and Climate Change
description: The atmosphere 302226 scales of motion, large-scale motion, general circulation, troposphere-stratosphere transport, atmospheric composition, global cycles and lifetimes. Atmospheric structure and thermodynamics 302226 pressure, density, composition, temperature structure, water in theatmosphere. Atmospheric transport 302226 Equations of atmospheric fluid mechanics, energy equation, turbulence, mixing lengthmodels, boundary layer processes, variation of wind speed with height. Atmospheric photochemistry and chemical kinetics 302226 tropospheric gas-phase, aerosol and cloud chemistry. Aerosol dynamics in the troposphere 302226 transport properties, thermodynamics and ucleation, dry and wet deposition. Cloud physics 302226 water equilibrium in the atmosphere, cloudand fog formation, cloud condensation nuclei, cloudprocessing of aerosols, ice clouds, rain, cloud climatology. Atmospheric radiation transfer 302226 solar and terrestrial radiation, energy balance for earth and atmosphere, light scattering and absorption, Rayleigh, Geometric and Mie theory, extinction and aerosol mixing state, radiative models for clear and cloudy atmospheres. Atmospheric chemical transport and general circulation models.

title: Public Policy and Governance in Technology and Development
