To look at different modes – imaginative, narrative, informative – using apt vocabularies Secondary objectives: 1. To realize that “inspiration” lies everywhere – in the daily news, in science journals in extra-curricular activities that interest students 2. To channelize those interests into writing practice so that each feed into the other 3. To make ongoing investigations of everyday realities and close observation of lived experiences a key to writing better 4. To see how vital our own environment, location and context is to shaping our creative process

title: An Introduction to the City
description: Our access to our city is mediated by technologies, systems, conventions and most of all, by our embodied limits. This course proposes to introduce the city as our prime repository of design problems. Its purpose is to introduce students to ways in which they can begin to understand urban problems. The city offers problems which are at once of a social and technical nature, and introducing students to this area of interdisciplinary scholarship will be helpful to them in developing their own future work.This course introduces students to the city through lectures, films and assignments. Students will learn to inquire into the city around them and the many systems which constitute it as a social and physical environment. Assignments and projects will involve field work and class work to describe, diagnose and propose interventions at a human scale.At the end of this course, students will be have learnt (a) to read the city in terms of the many systems which co-exist within it, (b) techniques of mapping urban phenomena, and (c) several theoretical and conceptual perspectives from which the city has been examined in the interdisciplinary literature

title: Concept Ideation in VR
description: Introduction to the course, scope, methodology a) Basic foundation course shall cover the introduction of VR / AR / MR system and educate the students about how does it work. Fundamental Sketching and getting started with gravity sketch (line work, shapes, basic transformations, interface overview, navigation, basic tools) a) Develop observation skills, identify key features, break complex 3D models in simple strokes and shapes. b) Basics of sketching, techniques and getting started with VR sketching software. c) Getting started with VR hardware and VR sketching software, necessary information about the interface and controllers. 3D sketching exercises and practice 3D Modeling Basics (extrusion, revolution, introduction to sculpting) a) Use of basic modeling functions (extrusion, revolution, etc.) b) Introduction to advanced modeling methods, for complex 3D models Introduction to Design principles (proportion, scale, symmetry, etc.) and their application in sketching and modeling in VR. Project Work

title: Emerging Technologies and Prototyping
description: Introduction to Emerging Technologies • Overview of the course objectives, structure, and expectations • Introduction to emerging technologies such as IoT, AI, and robotics in design • Case studies highlighting the impact of emerging technologies on various industries 1: Basics of Coding for Designers • Introduction to programming languages commonly used in design projects (e.g., Python, JavaScript) • Understanding Coding concepts and principles through Design concepts and principles • Pseudocode to Code through Ai • Integrated Development Environment (IDE) 2: Introduction to Embedded Systems • Understanding the fundamentals of embedded systems and their applications in design • Exploration of microcontrollers (e.g., Arduino, Raspberry Pi) and sensors commonly used in embedded design • Hands-on projects to create simple embedded systems prototypes 3: Mechanical Design for Emerging Technologies • Overview of mechanical design principles and their importance in emerging technology applications • Introduction to CAD software for 3D modeling and design (e.g., SolidWorks, Fusion 360) • Hands-on exercises to design and prototype mechanical components for emerging technology projects 4: Electrical and Electronic Technologies • Introduction to basic electrical and electronic concepts (e.g., circuits, components) • Hands-on experiments with breadboarding, soldering, and circuit design • Application of electrical and electronic principles to create simple circuits for embedded systems • Integration of sensors, actuators, and communication modules into design projects 5: Technology and User Experience • User experience (UX) design principles for emerging technology applications • Hands-on activities to apply design thinking and UX principles to project development 6: Prototyping and Testing • Overview of prototyping methods and tools (e.g., 3D printing, laser cutting) • Hands-on prototyping exercises to bring design concepts to life • Testing and iteration of prototypes to refine functionality and user experience Project Showcase and Reflection • Final presentation of student projects to peers and instructors • Reflection on the learning journey and personal growth throughout the course • Discussion on future directions and opportunities in the field of design and emerging technologies Throughout the course, students will work individually and in teams to apply the concepts and skills learned to develop proof-of-concept prototypes of their design ideas. Instructors will provide guidance, feedback, and support to facilitate learning and encourage creativity and innovation.

title: Human-Computer Interaction Design
description: Contents This is an introductory course on the design process of interactive products. The course will introduce user centred design methods such as contextual inquiry, analysing interviews, affinity diagrams, personas, scenarios, prototyping, and design evaluation. The course will also introduce some theoretical elements of human-computer interaction (HCI) such as the layers of user experience, modelling users with stages of use models, conceptual models and heuristics and principles of good interaction design. If time permits, the course may cover some related topics such as interaction techniques, usable security, user experience metrics, integrating HCI in software development and the history of HCI. Through the course, the students will get an opportunity to apply their learnings in a course project in which they will design an interactive product. Depending on the interest of the students, the product may either be a website, a mobile app or a hardware product. In this project, the students are expected to do fieldwork to identify problems, propose alternative designs, prototype one of them and evaluate it.

title: Stop motion and Experimental Animation
description: Learning Objectives Introduction to the Stop Motion Animation. To make them understand the balance between creative and technical execution. By combining engineering concepts with visual narrative, stop motion can inspire creativity in BTech students and promote a multidisciplinary approach to design thinking and problem-solving. Contents The evolution of Stop Motion Animation- Zoetrope Short stop motion films/videos. Behind the scenes/ case study of some films. Methodology/ Techniques Demo of Pixilation, Material Animation, sand animation, cut-out animation, Clay animation. Making of armatures Deliverables Pixilation, Different Material explorations- Animation clips. Zoetrope/Phenakistoscope, Basic Armature (human and Animal) Final Project- Camera Sliders/ Armatures with different materials( 3D printed), Visual Effects of Stop Motion (ocean, explosion, fire, smoke Software required in the Lab Dragonframe Studio

title: Introduction to Screen Printing
description: Learning Objectives Introduction to Screenprinting. The goal of this topic is to understand and acquire skills in screen printing, in order to expand students` skill base and provide students with a comprehensive understanding of the screen printing processes on different surfaces. Contents Explore its historical significance and applications of screen printing in design and commercial printing. Develop hands-on proficiency in stencil-making techniques, including paper stencils, photo emulsion, and direct block-out methods. Learn to set up screens, mix inks, and apply squeegee techniques. Explore multi-color printing, focusing on registration techniques. Explore surface variations, applying screen printing to paper, fabric, and other experimental materials. By the end of the course, students will develop a series of independent as well group work demonstrating both technical proficiency and conceptual growth through a series of finished screen prints. Deliverables A series of prints exploring composition, colour and printing surfaces A written reflection on process and conceptual developments A group project exploring a collective printing approach Equipment required in the Lab • Screen printing frames (various sizes), squeegees, photo emulsion and exposure unit, screen printing inks (for paper and fabric), tables set up for screen printing, paper and cloth, drying rack

title: Immersive Art in VR
description: Background Virtual Reality (VR) is reshaping the creative landscape, providing a canvas that goes beyond traditional limitations. Artists are no longer confined to flat surfaces or physical materials-they can paint in the air, sculpt in 360°, and animate immersive environments. By blending traditional artistic principles with cutting-edge digital tools, VR offers a revolutionary immersive and interactive expression platform. As digital tools increasingly influence creative disciplines, this course aims to equip students with the essential skills to adapt and innovate in this evolving artistic landscape. VR technology breaks down artistic barriers, enabling fully immersive experiences that open up new avenues for creativity, self-expression, and personal growth. Course Overview: Immersive Art in VR The "Immersive Art in VR" course is designed for students eager to explore art in a new dimension. It introduces participants to industry-leading VR creative tools, allowing them to experiment with painting, sculpting, animation, and calligraphy in a fully immersive environment. Students will learn how to harness the boundless potential of VR as an artistic medium, pushing the limits of imagination, interaction, and expression. Throughout the course, students will: Learn the fundamentals of VR art creation using industry-leading tools. Explore diverse artistic styles in VR, from dynamic 3D painting to virtual sculpting and animation. Create personal projects in VR, AR, or XR, choosing an art form of interest. Collaborate on group VR art installations to push creative and technical boundaries. This program delivers a transformative learning experience by leveraging VR`s unique capabilities, fostering creativity, innovation, and emotional expression. Additionally, the course incorporates creative art therapy principles, acknowledging the power of art as a means for enhancing emotional well-being and cognitive development. Learning Outcomes Upon completion of this course, students will: Master VR-based artistic tools to create digital paintings, 3D sculptures, and interactive animations. spaces. Expand creative problem-solving skills by working in 360° virtual Develop a portfolio of VR artworks, demonstrating expertise lin immersive media. Improve spatial reasoning and artistic visualization, critical for design, gaming, and AR/VR industries. Enhance emotional well-being through expressive creativity, drawing from art therapy principles. Engage in collaborative VR projects, strengthening communication and teamwork skills. Conclusion This course is more than just a new way to create art-it`s a gateway into the future of immersive creativity. By combining VR technology with expressivę artistic techniques, students will gain practical skills in digital artistry while benefiting from the therapeutic and cognitive advantages of immersive experiences. Whether students aim to explore art professionally, enter the XR industry, or simply unlock new creative possibilities, this course provides the tools and experience to push creative boundaries in the digital age.

title: Design Analysis
description: Importance of Design analysis for designers, Analysis of signs and symbols using semiotics, Analysis of Visuals using Communication Model, Analysis of products with Function-Behaviour-Structure ontology, Analysis of a visual narratives using narrative structures.

description: The studio project is offered for students who already have exceptional skills in the area of design and are able to contribute to the specialisation of the faculty. The student will have to undertake a topic in guidance with the guide from IDC and would have to complete the project within one semester.

title: Perspectives on World Cinema
description: An overview of Film HostorySocial Context of Filmamking :relationships of Filmmakers to their milieuAnalysis of Selected Classics of Cinema:Common themes and individuals styles, similiarities and contrasts in stylesAsian Cinema:Film directors Akira Kurosawa,Mizoguchi and KiarostamiEuropean Cinema:Film Directors Godard,Truffaut,Bergman and FelliniRussian Cinema:film directos Anderi TarkovskyAmeriacn Cinema:Film directors Sidney Lumet and Stanely KubrickWriting of a film diary of required film viewingA long form esssay on any filmmaker would be a final requirement for completion of the course

title: Sustainability Fundamentals
description: • Why critical, consumption vs population increases, challenges - Explore climate change, resource depletion, biodiversity loss, and their impact from design decisions • History of Sustainability movement, Paris accord, COP initiative, Regulatory and policy evolution (including for India). • Foundational Sustainability concepts – Triple bottom line (environmental, social and economic) considerations, circular economy, cradle to cradle design and life cycle impact. • Design for Sustainability (across Product, Systems, Services, communication, Mobility, UI/UX,) covering approaches of Material and process selection, Longevity, renewable sources, Servicification, business impact and strategy planning and integration, Biomimicry. • User behaviour and communication: Understand how design can influence user behaviour towards sustainable choices and responsible consumption, Consumer perspective, Socio-cultural perspectives. • Tools and Frameworks – Planetary boundaries, SDG, Net Zero, 9R approach, Circularity levers. Measurement standards, • Case studies and real world applications, Subject matter experts, minor and major projects to apply learnings (including on other semester projects)

title: Physical Ergonomics
description: Introduction to the concept of Ergonomics in Design, Analysis of Man Machine Environment system Design . Physical aspects of Ergonomics including anthropometrics, human body-structure and function, posture, joint movement and biomechanics; Issues of cognition, perception and performance . Ergonomics and Workplace Safety, Occupational health and Musculoskeletal disorders; Physical environment and their impact on human performance, Application of Physical Ergonomics principles in real-world scenarios with respect to Product and Vehicle Design

title: Visual and Cognitive Ergonomics
description: Human cognitive process including perception, attention, memory, decision-making, and problem-solving and it’s influence on human performance; Human physical characteristics and limitations with respect to visual communication; Visual ergonomics related to  readability issues in different contexts (e.g., static/dynamic, short/long distance, type of display etc.); Human perception related to visual design application, Heuristic evaluation and UX audits; Stress and mental workload; Implication and application of cognitive principles to design interfaces with respect to Interaction and Communication Design.

title: Global Design Thoughts and Discourse
description: . Understanding ‘Form follows nature’, ‘Form follows Function’ and ‘Form follows emotion’ . Understanding the concept of ‘Less is more’ . The role of aesthetics in society . The role of design in a sustainable world . Design in the context of a globalised world . Exposure to Indian and Asian thoughts on design

title: Product Interface Design
description: • Investigations and study of visual, functional and ergonomic requirements of control and display interfaces. Legibility of display elements, character of different typefaces and their readability. • Study of the process of building interactions. • The course involves an interdisciplinary approach including User Centered Design Process, Activity Analysis, Structuring of Content, Participatory Design, Experiential Ideation, Scenario Building, Linear and Animatic storyboarding, Soft Physical Prototyping Techniques. • The students will collaboratively design an interactive product interface.

title: Presentation and Communication Skills

Creative writing, articulation, narration exercises. Final Project: Documentation and presentation exercise.

Study of 3D abstraction in art and sculpture. Exploration of industrial material and processes as elements of design through 3D abstraction of entities in Nature.

Exploration of form to develop imagination and insight. Use of metaphors to generate new forms. Concept of family of forms. Introduction to 3D geometry. Basic 3D Forms: cube, tetrahedron, octahedron etc. And their imaginative use in generating complex forms and structures. Use of combinatrics as a method of 3D form generation. Form, material and process relationship.

title: Product Design -2
description: • The emphasis of the course is on group design projects. Selection of the projects is based on the possibility of user interaction leading to innovation. Projects end with a comprehensive presentation through working/mock up models, design drawing and a report. • The project is supported by detailed discussion on various stages in the design process emphasizing the complementary nature of systematic and creative thinking. • This is achieved by short supporting assignment in following topics: Creativity techniques like brain storming & synectics to develop creative attitude and open mind, design opportunity, problem perception, Idea Sketching , clustering of ideas for concept development, exploratory mock up models for concept development, evaluation of concepts, final concept selection, concept development, refinement and detailing.

Influence of materials and processes on product aesthetics. • Industrial finishes for plastic, wood and metals. • Properties and use of rubber, ceramics and glass. Properties of nature materials like wood, bamboo, cane, leather, cloth, jute and paper and their use at craft and industrial levels

Studio Lighting methods and available light photography (with practicals) Study of the works of Masters of photography Study of different genres: Portrait photography, Street Photography, Product Photography and Photography as Art.

title: Advanced Product Design
description: 1. Product function, structure, form, and ergonomic relationship. 2. Situation/context of use, users, market research, and product research with a focus on materials and processes. 3. Analysis of research information and identification of problem areas leading to a problem statement and articulation of constraints. 4. Methods/techniques for the evolution of creative alternative concepts. 5. Validation of concepts through exploratory mock-ups. 6. Finalization of the concept, preparation of the final model, technical drawings, and other supporting documentation.

title: Elements of Design
description: An introduction to basic elements: Line, texture, colour, form, symmetry, balance, scale, mass, unity and variety.Concept of visual language and visual designIntroduction to Gestalt Laws, composition and figure and ground relationship.Introduction to concept of negative space.Use of symmetry. Generation of patterns and textures using simple elements.Introduction to typography and fonts.Use of grids in graphic composition.Colour circle, colour combinations and its dimensions: hue, value and chroma.Colour meanings in traditions and psychological use of colours.

title: Design Issues
description: This course will contain two predominant approaches. The first is reflective which will help students to ponder on the very basic yet subjective questions like what makes a good designer. • It will address understanding perceptions & Paradigms which enable students to “think outside the box” by identifying the boxes one creates inadvertently while designing. • It will deal with the tangible and intangible relevance of broadening one’s perspectives in Arts Aesthetics, Science and Technology to design. The second is an exposure to different thoughts and perspectives, concerns and issues in the context of design. • The challenges in design, layers or user experience, design process • Design for the real world • It will also expose the students to emerging areas of design, • Relevance of design in the context of India, • Importance of sustainable design practices, preserving traditional practices & designing fo r the underserved communities. • Lastly it will provide an exposure to the design process.

title: Visual Language Syntactics
description: Relationship between syntactic, semantics and pragmatics. Viewpoints, point of reference and framing. Relationship of colour, form and meaning. Explorations in visual abstraction. Study of Visual Language – critical study of visual elements, features and principles. Exploration and creation of complex and meta patterns. Analysis of Aesthetics- the structure of Appearance. Form in nature, Exploration of visual images with analogies from nature.

title: Design Workshop

Study of various techniques used in Design execution like Calligraphy, Pottery and Ceramics, Printmaking, Theatre, Film and Video, Performing Arts Sculpture, Painting, Story Telling and Narrative, Toy Design, Book Design, Sound Design, Exhibition Design, Information Graphics and Way finding, Environment etc.

title: Studies in Typography

Theoretical and applicable principles of communicating with letterforms. Multi lingual Typography. Expressive Typography. Compositions with type. Calligraphic Experiments in Indian Language scripts. Exploration of three dimensional features of letter forms. Typography in different contexts like Books, Mailers, New media, Posters, Signages, Motion graphics etc. Study of grids and layouts.

title: Introduction to User Studies
description: • Observation • Contextual inquiry • Interpreting interviews with team members, • Consolidating data across users with personas • Using data in the design process

title: Semantics and Communication Theory

Human information processing strategies with emphasis on processing of visual information. Introduction to visual perception and Gestalt laws of organization. Introduction to information theory and their application to spatial and spatio – temporal message design. • Concept of attention in perception. Relationship between message design and attention, Exploring relationships between the semantics and the structure of messages. • Course is supported by studio work which uses these theories in design of communication messges.

title: The Speaking Image
description: Keyframes from the history of visual art Keyframes from the history of literature The past, the present and the future of the image The illustrated text The illuminated image

Handling print projects through digital media. Digital format image manipulation, Colour correction, font management, colour proofing etc.

title: Introduction to Animation
description: • Introduction to animation, processes, techniques & Styles. • Applications of animation – Data Visualisation, Gaming, Information graphics, Motion graphics. • Hands on exposure through assignments. 10.

Design methodology –Study in the phases of process from analysis through synthesis an evaluation. To be able to develop a program for investigation of problem. To be able to think from the audience point of view and to construct communicable messages for the audience through various media.

description: • Introduction to Ergonomics / human factors • The concept of Systems Design. How to look at the design issues • Use of Anthropometry in design • The Neural systems • Sensation and perception • Cognitive psychology • Human information processing and execution • Hick’s law, Fitts’s Law, Miller number • Different types of Controls & Displays and it organization. • Final design Assignment

title: Indian Thought and Tradition
description: Visual Art – Traditional and contemporary schools of Indian Art, folk art Oral Traditions from the ancient to the present Music – Classical (Hindustani & Carnatic), Semi-classical, Folk, Fusion Dance – Classical, Folk, Contemporary Theatre – Traditional (Natashastra), Contemporary, Puppet Theatre Science – An understanding of the scientific approach to everything and the holistic approach to it and how it influenced all aspects like health, architecture, management and even the arts. Post independence understanding of India as a nation and bridging the wealth of the past with the potential of the future. Reflection of this exposure to the question – What is or what can be called “Indian” Design

title: Interaction Design
description: • Design process and methodology for designing solutions for interactive products, services and events: Design of integrated systems, products for future use, products to be used in groups, devices used in public places, design of tangible, gestural and expressive interfaces, products that enrich user experience • The course takes an inter-disciplinary approach drawing upon product design, visual communication, information architecture, cognitive psychology and computer science. The course involves exploration of alternative, pushing the envelope of what is known. • The focus is on working collaboratively in groups to solve design problems. The course will involve doing projects. Students need to build soft prototypes of proposed systems at the end of the course. • The course involves learning to critically analyze thoughts and conduct independent Research, to be able to work in teams and collaborate to solve problems, to build confidence in being creative and innovative and be able to think critically. • The course also introduces best practice of documentation and presentation – both oral and written.

title: Introduction to Photography
description: Lens, Focal Length, Exposure (Shutter speed, Aperture, ISO), Depth of Field, Histogram, Picture Styles, RAW image, Digital Imaging, Sensor, Crop Factor, Light Metering (Incident, Reflected), Photographic Documentation.

title: Trends in Interactive Technologies
description: • History of technology development, influences on society and design • Information and communication technology, past, present and future • Influences from other media • New trends in interaction design hardware and software • Interaction design in the context of India • Design for future needs.

description: History of Typography, Type Terminology, Font Classification, Anatomy of Typefaces, Introduction to Indic Script Typography, Typesetting, Web Typography.

title: Acting and Staging
description: Acting and Stating are absolute essentials for making the characters and story to come alive. • Introduction to stagecraft for theatre, live action film and animation • Understanding characters and their back stories • Interpreting the importance of the scene through action & staging • Acting workshop • Using editing and understanding film language to enhance character development and storytelling.

timetable.professor: Shyam Subhashrao Wanare, Sumant Muralidhar Rao

description: Colour Theory, Colour through different mediums, Physical properties of Colour, Colour Schemes, Colour Harmonies, Colour Symbolism. Methods of Composition: Realistic, Decorative, Abstract Compositions etc. Principles of Organization & Composition: Repetition, Variety, Contrast, Radiation, Rhythm, Balance, Gradation, Dominance & Subordination, Transition, Harmony, Unity etc.

description: Object Creation for Animation - Creating and Modifying Objects - The main animation parameters - Grouping and Hierarchy - Optimizing your projects and Interactivity for animation creation Creating Animation - Project Planning, Execution to Completion - Keyframes, Interpolation and Graph Editing Compositing and Special Effects - Creating a Special Effects Film – Script to screen - Influence of animation on Live action Films: previz, animation, compositing and effects. - Advanced digital techniques in animation

description: • Understanding contemporary trends in animation making in terms of content, styling, techniques and applications • Studying animation films through film viewing, appreciation, criticism, theoretical writings, essays , research studies • Aesthetics: Introduction to Aesthetics (Indian and Western) • Introduction to major art movements • Narrative: Introduction to narrative structures (Indian and Western) • Modes of Narrative • Narrative Discourse • Film Theory: Perception • Representation • Signification • Genres • Identification and Psychoanalysis • Interpretation

title: User Studies
description: Gathering user data through observation and contextual interviews Interpreting interviews with team members, creating work models (Flow, sequence, culture, physical, artefact etc.) Consolidating data across users with techniques such as affinity diagrams and personas Using data in the design process Comparison of contextual interview to other techniques such as survey research, focus groups, Rapid Assessment Procedure, task analysis.

title: Animation Principles-2
description: • Study of Animation Techniques & styles developed and used over the years in animation. • Appreciating the difference between the processes of the basic 3 methods of animation, namely • frame by frame creation of animation (like traditional 2D) • Modification of object or image to produce animation. (like paint on glass, sand on glass, simple claymation without armatures etc) • Manipulation of objects to produce animation. (like 2D cutout animation, 3D claymation with armatures, simple object animation, Puppets, etc) • Exploring & developing the animation world based on the technique you might choose for animation. • The Digital World and the avenues opened by it in terms of animation design. Animation articulation and performance • Understanding the role of drama and acting in creating convincing characterization and emotion • Creating believability by taking off on the natural, making objects behave in character • Expressing attitude, gesture, body language • Role playing through actual and mental miming • Character acting, reacting, take • Expressing exaggeration through caricatured matter acted upon by caricatured forces Character Animation • Reflecting on human movement • Gaining an insight into creating believable action • Practicing the mechanics of biped walks, runs, head turns, gestures • Methods of lip-syncing to sound breakdowns • Posing characters in scenes Animal movement • Reflecting on animal movement • Gaining an insight into creating believable action • Practicing the mechanics of quadraped- walks, runs, gallops; bird movement and flight Sfx • An awareness of and an ability to create convincing atmosphere • Creating animated environments and atmosphere like flames, smoke, water, rain, snow etc. to offset principal animation • Creating Special effects such as explosions, speed lines etc.

timetable.professor: Phani Prasad Rao Tetali, Shyam Subhashrao Wanare

title: Usability Evaluation
description: Elements of user experiencePrinciples, heuristics, patterns, guidelines and standards of human-computer interactionSetting user experience goals for a productgHeuristic evaluationUsability evaluation, think aloud protocol, card sorts and user performance testsRecruitment and design of usability testsIntroduction to quantitative evaluation techniques.

title: Animation Design-2
description: • Experimenting with techniques for visual storytelling, structure, story building, writing for animation • Examining indigenous narratives, both contemporary and traditional to gain an understanding of storytelling methods pertinent to our culture • Creating dramatic flow, planning, pacing, sequencing, organizing visual flow and continuity in storyboards • Co-ordinating character, scene length, pace of action • Choreographing scene change and camera movement • Planning, examining, analyzing and pacing character behaviour • An exposure to various animation techniques • Workshops using both 2D and 3D techniques on Computer, Film, Video etc. • Production of professional stop-motion animation puppet and usable set props. • Understanding how a stop-motion animation production works • Sculpting, mould making, armature construction and casting techniques. • Methods to fabricate a stop-motion animation puppet • Techniques for set construction. • Doping, animating, lighting and shooting • Post production techniques

Study of how people sense, perceive, understand, use and experience interactive objects and spaces.

title: Sketching and Anatomy-2
description: • To become fluent with capturing the human and animal form • Proportions, structure, volume and shading techniques.

title: Digital Animation-1
description: The Digital Image - Analog v/s Digital and the Binary world - Digital Colour, Channels, bit depth and resolutions - Bitmap and Vector graphics - Boolean operations and Blend modes Getting Real world into the Digital Realm - Input tools like digital photography, video, digital intermediate (DI) for film, scanning and 3D digitizing - Paint and Photo retouching tools Organising your data - Data management tools - Organization of Research with Mindmaps and Story Concept Creation - Spreadsheets Animation and the Digital world - Tradigital Animation - 3D Animation - Other methods of Animation Digital Filmmaking - Compositing and Special Effects - Editing - Sound Engineering Other Digital Technologies - Motion Control - Motion Capture - Motion Tracking - Image Processing, Matting and Keying - Use of various technological concepts like solid dynamics, fluid dynamic, fractals etc. in animation. title: Animation Principles-1
description: • Understanding principles that translate sequential images into action to make animation believable • Understanding properties of matter • Making use of the wave principle, delayed secondary action, slow and fast action impact , speed, weight, tendency of weight to move in a certain way, recoil effects, squash and stretch related to weight, overlapping action, follow through • Animating force acting on objects , object weight, construction, flexibility, object behavior when force acts on them • Principles of Timing • Gaining an insight into the invisible concept of time in nature • Understanding the basic unit of time in animation • Emphasizing the difference between caricature, drama, humor • Timing governing acting and movement • The use of anticipation, action, reaction • Methods of doping, writing exposure sheets, bar sheets • Planning accents, beats, scene timing, spacing of drawings, holds, easing in and out • Animating to music • Principles of Movement • Understanding the meaning of movement and movement in nature and what movement expresses • Awareness of how mood and feeling can be conveyed through movement and animate and inanimate object behavior • Examining the laws of motion in the context of animation; cause and effect, thrown objects, rotating, force, oscillating movement, friction, resistance • Studying the tendency of weight to move in a particular manner • Simplification and exaggeration of movement

title: Animation Design-1
description: Covers the entire process from script to screen in process for an animation film (short or feature) • Overview of the Process • Preproduction, Production, Post Production • A comparison between live action filmmaking& animation The primary difference being that an animation film tends to be pegged down pretty accurately by the end of the preproduction process itself whereas for a live action film it is close to completion only at the end of editing which is a post production process. Preproduction • Story to Script • Whether adapted or original, taking the story from a verbal or spatial medium like a book or graphic novel to a form suitable for making a film. What makes a good story? • Plot & Character: Action Plots & Mind Plots. Analysis of different types of plots • Archetypes v/s Stereotypes - understanding of archetypes and a brief introduction to the monomyth (hero’s journey). • Why Animation? (instead of live action) • Animation as a story telling (narrative) medium • Animation as an artistic medium • Visual Concepts, Character Exploration & Storyboards • Animatics • Character Design & Model Sheets, Layouts & Scene Planning Production • Animation • Acting - Animation is not about moving images but moving people • Traditional 2D animation • a. Linetests - Keyframes & Timing (breakdowns & inbetweens) also brief introduction to thumbnailing (for quick exploration of alternative methods of animating a scene) • Study comparisons between different rhythms of animation • Executing straight ahead and inbetweened planned animation, limited animation, full animation • Drawing key frames, breakdowns, in between, animation cycles • Cleanups Post Production • Voice, Music & Effects • Understanding the dynamics of sound design and use of sound as a key component of animation • Designing a sound track for animation including • music, dialogue, voice overs, lip synch and FX • Recording and mixing multiple tracks • Post processing sound • Compositing & Editing • Work in Progress. Development of a film from Animatic to Edit with different scenes at different stages. • Coming together of the various elements of the scene. • Final Mixing of Sound & Final Edit • Understanding the dynamics of camera moves and magnifications • Experimenting with camera techniques and working directly under camera • Using registration fields, table moves and peg movements • Screening & Feedback Visual Form • Exploring the look and feel for animation through concept art • Planning character design, layout design, illustration style, composition, staging, backgrounds • A study of indigenous design and painting, both contemporary and traditional to understand and analyze a variety of styles and visual language

title: Design and Social Entrepreneurship
description: Role of Design in Social Entrepreneurship Social Impact and Commercial return Gender and Social Entrepreneurship Design for Change Ideation & business model for Entrepreneurial Venture Design plays a major role in social change. Designing a physical product or preparing a communication material can be applications for a proposition for a social change. Example: An easy adoptable and low cost hand washing station can substantially decrease the child mortality and maternity mortality caused due to unhygienic environments. A new kind of ploughing machine can be user-friendly to women farmers of Rajasthan, and, hence, encourage women to take up farming. These examples can only see the day of light if these are converted into ventures through social entrepreneurship. The course will deal with the fundamentals social entrepreneurship. The class room component of the course will expose students to how a design input can solve the social problems. The classroom teaching will also help students to revisit the age-old thinking of “social venture” and reposition it as a commercial viable social proposition. The class room course will also consist of several presentations and case studies on how today’s social change and commercialization and will include a project/practical component. The end component of the course will train students in locating ideas for social entrepreneurship and building a business plan to convert the idea into a social business.

title: Instructional Design

title: Sustainable Design
description: Understanding the domain of needs and values. Examine the history and development of sustainability as a social goal through 3P diagram and explore its implications for the design of built environments. Study concepts such as life-cycle analysis both in manmade systems and in nature, biomimicry, natural capitalism, cradle-to-cradle production, unfolding wholeness, and ecological design. In addition to the latest tools, techniques, and materials, you explore government programs, legislation, industry regulations and codes, green certifications, LEED, and nonprofit organizations involved with sustainability. Solve design problem in sustainability context and experience how a new window on innovation opens up. Translate your knowledge of sustainable design concepts into practical, deployable, and tangible methods. Learn to take responsibility for your work by understanding the impact that your designs have on the environment and how to control this impact. Through research of materials, systems, and manufacturing / construction methods, you explore ways to design and build in an environmentally responsible manner. Using case studies, you look at how others are creating communities, solving economic problems, and developing new solutions to design. Class involves lectures, discussions, and student presentations exploring these ideas.

title: Human Factors in Technological Systems
description: Advanced issues in Human Factors The sensory basis of Human Factors The cognitive basis of Human Factors The physical basis of Human Factors Human Factors and the Environment Research Methods in Human Factors Work Analysis Methods in Human Factors Design Methods in Human Factors Display and Control Design I Display and Control Design II Organizational Human Factors Human Factors and risk management in large-scale systems Human Factors in Accident Investigation Human Systems Integration

title: Handmade Character Design
description: This course will introduce the basics of character development. In order to understand the fundamentals of effective character design, students will research and evaluate iconic characters from the world of narratives. The course covers the history and development of puppets and mascots, using important case studies, research undertaken and current best practices in this domain. How individuals and organizations use the language of design to develop conceptual attributes of successful identity design would be studied. While developing your own characters you consider factors such as intended audience, visual impact, exaggeration of features, scale, material and purpose The practical component focuses on the use of hand and power tools to explore various materials and techniques. The intensive hands-on processes which may include modelling, carving, molding, casting, assembling, stitching, etc. To execute tangible character along with the documentation, students will create backstories, props and environments to both fill out and fine tune their Designs. Students may choose to write an optional research paper on their final outcome if they feel that research and writing supports and extends their studio practice.

title: Design Exploration Seminar I
description: The seminar involves student exploring in an area related to design and is expected to build an artefact/prototype of the same on the topic over a period of three months (December to February). In this period, students are expected to dedicate a period equivalent to about 8 weeks. Students need to choose a topic suggested by a faculty member and work under their guidance. The work may be single or in groups. The size of the group determines the expectations of the outcome. The work may involve literature review, defining a research question, designing experiments or studies to answer a research question and articulation of the findings. The work is expected to result in a physical exhibit. The outcome will be evaluated through a single blind peer review process chaired by two faculty members in IDC.

title: Sketching for Communication Design
description: Sketching/Drawing tools and techniques x Observation and sketching of animal/human anatomy, botanical details x Visual representations of objects, spaces, and environment through light and shadow and perspective x Visualization of characters, situations, abstract concepts and Ideas x Composition of visual elements

title: Theoretical Perspectives in Design
description: This course will introduce students to theoretical perspectives and methods used in the critical design process. The course would introduce various theoretical perspectives in design in relation to the socio-cultural issues of the present, structured around thematic areas of investigation. Students will learn to use theoretical lenses to define, analyse, and explain design artefacts. Through these lenses, students will explore questions such as, How does design and the designer interact with specific social phenomena? How do they influence each other? and, How are existing social structures mediated and propagated through design? Specific theories that the course will cover include: Semiotics and Communication, Labour and Work, Language and Power, Gender and Gendering, Universalism and Regionalism etc. Over all This course will guide students to: • Analyse design interventions through various historical and theoretical lenses. • Develop awareness towards diverse narratives of power, identity, representation, technology, sustainability, agency, and several other such issues. • Engage in critical dialogue to understand the social, cultural, environmental, and ethical implications of design objects and practices. Throughout the course, real-life examples will be used to illustrate and explain theories.

title: Advanced Topics in Service Design

Introduction to service domains such as healthcare, education and financial services (all with emphasis on socially relevant services)

title: Experimental Film
description: • Introduction to experimental films • Cinema of attraction and early experiments (a lecture on early cinema of attraction and other experiments that evolved the medium) • Surrealism in films • Rhythm over reason • Early avant garde films • Lyrical film • Structural films • Indian experimental films • Pitching idea for experimental film (ideation, writing and storyboarding) • Preproduction of selected pitch (location scouting, production design, test shoots) • Principal photography • Editing, sound design and packaging • Screening and reflection

title: Screen Printing: Craft, Expression, Reproduction
description: Learning Objectives: Gain practical skills in screen prepařation, stencil-making, inking, and multi-layer printing. Explore experimental processes while understanding the aesthetic and material possibilities of screen printing across a variety of surfaces. Apply screen printing to multiple design contexts, including visual communication, textiles, product surfaces, and prototyping. Translate ideas into reproducible formats while learning to embrace the value of iteration and variation.Develop both independent projects and collaborative work that combines technical precision with conceptual exploration. Contents of the course: Cultivate reflective practice through process documentation and material testing.Contents Contextual grounding: Understanding the history, evolution, and ongoing relevance of screen printing in contemporary design practice, with examples from visual, material, and applied contexts.Technical foundations: Mastery of tools, materials, and core printing techniques, including stencil-making, screen preparation, inking, registration, and multi-layer printing.Surface and application explorations: Applying screen printing across a variety of surfaces and materials, including paper, textiles, and experimental substrates, while integrating digital and hybrid workflows.Experimental practices: Using iteration, improvisation, scale, layering, and hybrid processes to drive creative exploration and innovation within print-based projects.Reflection and documentation: Maintaining process journals, visual and written reflections, and participating in critiques to consolidate learning, track experimentation, and support conceptual development.DeliverablesSeries of individual prints exploring composition, colour, layering, and surface experimentation.Process documentation and written reflections highlighting experimentation and conceptual development.Collaborative group project demonstrating collective exploration and innovative application of screen printing. (Optional) Curated portfolio showcasing technical proficiency, conceptual growth, and adaptability across design contexts.Equipment required in the LabScreen printing frames (various sizes), squeegees, photo emulsion and exposure unit, screen printing inks (for paper, fabric, etc), tables set up for screen printing, paper and cloth, drying rack

title: Explainer Video Design

(Discussing formal elements like staging, shot taking, editing, sound, narrative structures of the video) • Thinking about media in terms of interaction, screen size, audio etc. • Production (filming, sound design, editing and graphics) • Titles, keywords, description and thumbnails

title: Basics of Entrepreneurship for Designers
description: What is Design Entrepreneurship/Intrapreneurship (E/I), why important now, what is E/I mindset, resilience, creativity/problem solving, agility, and risk taking.  Opportunity canvas - Product/Service/Social enterprise using Design as a core differentiator – identifying specific needs and drivers for each.  Fundamental building blocks: o Understanding Value creation/capture through Design o Customer centricity and their understanding – Target Group, Motivations, decision making, and value systems o Futuring – Design and consumer megatrends, forecasting, PESTLE, scenario building, use of technology  Introduction to Design Thinking as an approach to navigate complex emerging problems which could form the foundation of a new enterprise/project.  Design case studies and real world applications, Subject matter experts, experience sharing, minor and major projects to apply learnings (including on other semester projects)

title: Interaction Design for Development
description: The course aims to motivate people (designers, engineers and managers) to work on problems of people in developing countries, and particularly on problems that can be solved with the help of interactive products. In the first half, the course will provide an overview of the past and current trends in interaction design for development through lectures. Some of these lectures will be based on research done in IDC, while some lectures will be by HCI4D researchers from different universities around the world. (Some of these lectures will be given by people based in a different time zone, and may be held in the evenings based on the convenience.) Topics:  An overview of technology adoption in India and other developing countries  User-usage model of technology adoption by emergent users  Hierarchical navigation of information systems by emergent users  Audio-visual interfaces and their usability  Text input interfaces for Indian scripts  Supporting health with information and communication technology (ICT)  Role of ICTs in supporting education  Digital security for emergent users  Gender and ICTs  Special groups within developing countries – disabled, elderly and children  Experiments and lessons learnt in HCI4D from around the world In the second half, the students will work on design projects related to a HCI4D topic for emergent users. The students are expected to make an intermediate presentation and a final presentation outlining their project work.

title: Interactive Data Visualization
description: • Information visualization overview, historical perspective • Vision, perception and cognition principles of information visualisation • Data principles, models, dimensions and analysis • Information taxonomy • Visual encoding of data • Mapping, cartography, geo-visualization • Visualization tools and strategis • Data stories and presentation

title: Story and Narrative
description: The intent of this course is to introduce students to broad categories of narratives, Indian and others, their structure and models, working with both verbal and visual storytelling. Students will be encouraged to explore large questions such as how did evolution shape our minds to see the world in narrative terms? Does fiction have meaning beyond indulgence or pleasure? They will be pressed to pursue more specific ones that elucidate the relationship between narrative and narrative devices. They will learn craft through group and individual projects, hone writing skills, study space and time in literature, and bring to bear analytic instruments from a variety of disciplines including culture studies, literary criticism, anthropology, folklore, history, among others, to negotiate the deceptively simple terrain of story and narrative.

title: Design Research Methodologies
description: Introduction to qualitative research methods used in design, Introduction to quantitative methods, exploratory, inferential and casual research, Research Design and Introduction to Reading research and Reporting research.

title: Introduction to Interface Design
description: ● Elements of User Interface Design viz. Colour, Typography, Images, Composition, Icons etc. ● Methods and approaches to User Interface Design ● Interaction Principles ● Interface Evaluation ● Designing for multiple media ● Microinteractions ● Ethical issues around new and emerging technologies

title: Sound and Camera
description: • Understanding the dynamics of sound design and use of sound as a key • Designing a sound track for animation including music, dialogue, voice overs, lip synch and FX • Recording and mixing multiple tracks • Post processing sound • Understanding the dynamics of camera moves and magnification • Experimenting with camera techniques and working directly under camera • Using registration fields, table moves and peg movements.

title: Design Evaluation
description: Elements of user experience Principles, heuristics, patterns, guidelines and standards of human-computer interaction, Setting user experience goals for a product, Heuristic evaluation Usability evaluation, think aloud protocol, card sorts and user performance tests Recruitment and design of usability tests Introduction to quantitative evaluation techniques. Introduction to Predictive models, such as KLM-GOMS. Hick’s law and Fitts’s law. Design evaluation in different contexts and different sectors connected to Interaction Design

title: Animation Production
description: • The workflow in animation studios • Project effort estimation and costs • Project pitch • “Bible” for proposed production • Types of production – short format, long format, episodic, mini-series, etc. • An exposure to marketing and distribution • Intellectual property rights, co-productions, etc.

title: Immersive Media Design
description: Content: The theory part of the course will cover the following: 1. History of Immersive Media (XR) a. Evolution of Communication Media b. Evolution of XR 2. Present Scenario a. XR & Technology b. How & Why XR Experiences Work? c. Applications of XR d. Design Processes: i. Interactive Experiences (VR/AR/MR) ii. 360 Narratives 3. Future of Immersive Media a. Emerging Technologies connected to XR b. Potential applications of XR The students would be guided towards designing applications/experiences for Virtual Reality, Augmented Reality and VR Films. There will be a strong emphasis on hands-on work, and students will be expected to create working prototypes. Evaluation: • Participation in the class • Project presentation • Quality of the prototypes

title: Seminar and Critical Writing
description: • Encouraging students to prepare a seminar presentation and write an academic report on vehicle design related or any topic from wide variety of areas like technology, design, architecture, humanities, arts etc. • Demands and conventions of academic reading and writing. • Analyzing tests, using evidence and secondary source material • Citations and referencing. • From prewriting exercises through rough drafts and revisions.

title: Design Fiction
description: Objective: This course, through the integration of findings from critical readings, ethnographic research, mediated storytelling and hybrid prototyping, will speculate, imagine and prototype plausible futures. The course seeks to produce a diversity of “what will?” and “what if?” cultural provocations that deeply examine possible, desirable and undesirable futures. By the end of the course, students will be able to: • Gain knowledge of prior and related future-focused work and speculative/critical/fiction design. • Gain the ability to read and discuss research papers from the design fiction literature. • Gain an understanding of the key techniques of inversion, defamiliarization, uncertainty scenarios, and everyday practice to create objects, systems and experiences that create a discursive space for conversation, debate and understanding. • Produce diegetic prototypes using various media and storytelling techniques. • Articulate and critique the social and cultural, ethical and legal, health and environmental, political, and economic implications of emerging technologies. • Develop evaluation/critique tools and frameworks. Content: • Introduction to Design Fiction, Critical Design and Speculative Design, citing examples from architecture, film, art, and literature. • Introduction to futurisms in theory and fiction • Survey of new media and emerging technologies • Survey of narrative and production techniques used for creating Design Fiction, drawing on historical and recent works to see the methods in action. • Socio-Technical Systems • Narrative structures and storytelling techniques • Diegetic prototyping Methodology: Classes will involve lectures, discussions, studio sessions, project presentations, critique sessions and occasional guest presentations. Students will participate in and lead in-class discussions and presentations. The students will be required to create Design Fiction artefact(s) to be presented at the end of the course. Evaluation: • Individual reading and discussion assignment • Individual project

title: Pattern Language for Interaction Design
description: • The theoretical aspects of patterns and pattern language to be covered: concept of patterns, ways of writing patterns, organising principles, distinction between a pattern collection and a pattern language, creation of pattern languages, uses of pattern languages, application of pattern languages. Critiques of the concept. Introduction to pattern languages in the domain of interaction design and interface design. Studio work will comprise several (Likely number: 3) assignments pertaining to the topics covered. One assignment would center on successful application of the chosen interaction design pattern language to a complex interactive system (software/hardware/combination)

title: Service Design
description: • Importance of Services in today’s world, Product-services continuum, nature of services, relevance of design thinking in conceptualising and developing services, unique service design context that influence design, interaction and experience as source of value, overlaps of service design with user experience design – applicability of methodologies and methods, service experience design methods and techniques (e.g. service ecosystems maps, service blueprinting...), designing for technology based services and self service, designing service recovery encounters, service design outcomes (touch points, service encounters, service systems...), service prototyping and testing, aspects of service experience that influence the service quality, brief overview of service system deployment, operations and management. 10.

title: Design for Future
description: 1. Introduction to the basics of Design Forecasting, need and importance 2. Different stages and requirements of design forecasting process 3. Introduce different research methods used to carry out design forecasting 4. Exposure to DeF framework, TRIZ, trend mapping, scenario building and a few other design forecasting methods 5. How to develop a foresight: gather data, make observations, develop insights, make a projection 6. Project work: consciously create effective and efficient design brief(s) needed for the future by using (DeF) framework, develop futuristic concepts, make presentation posters

title: Computer Game Design
description: This is an elective course that gives exposure to students about game design and game art. It is an introduction to Game Design, Detailed Design Docs, Storytelling, Visual Storytelling, Critical Game Analysis. It includes • Various Genres of Games • Various platforms in games and their differences • Game Art and a comparison with Art asset creation for animation • Game Art production techniques and technologies involved • Technology for game development (a study on various game engines) • A detailed look at a 3D game engine • Game design Documents and Technical Design Document • Level Design • Sound, UI Design • Production pipelines in game production • The gaming industry, Producing and Distribution • Making a playable level 10.

title: Power of Colour
description: * Some aspects that will be covered in the course include: It begins with the fundamentals of colour theory and perception, helping students understand how colour influences visual impact, emotional response, and user experience. The module explores colour psychology, cultural meanings, and global market preferences, enabling students to design with sensitivity to diverse audiences. * Students will study how colour supports brand identity and market positioning, with case studies from iconic automotive brands. The course also integrates the study of Colour, Material, and Finish (CMF), focusing on how colour interacts with texture, light, and form. Trends and forecasting are introduced through resources like BASF and Axalta. * Through workshops and critiques, students will apply their knowledge by creating brand-specific colour palettes and CMF strategies for concept vehicles. The module emphasises practical skills and strategic thinking to prepare students for industry-relevant CMF design roles.

title: Representation Techniques for Animation
description: This course gives an opportunity for students to experiment with different representation Techniques for their project films • Different techniques of representations for animation. • Experiment with different methods of representation • Exploring different skills, tools and medias to represent for animation

title: Computational Thinking in Design
description: Topics Include: Introduction: computers, computation, JavaScript, p5js, Processing, Python, IDEs, drawing things, making things move, Types, Variables, functions, objects, classes. Modes of abstraction, representation Randomness, Random walks, determinism, Monte Carlo processes, Diffusion, Bernoulli & Poisson processes, Texture as a visual signature of randomness. Noise Visualization libraries, UIs, interactivity Vectors, Forces, Motion, Change, Particle Systems, Inanimate collectives Autonomous Agents, Vehicles, Walkers, Agent Based Systems, Animate collectives, decentralization Fractals, L Systems, Cellular Automata, Rewriting systems Spatial organizations: grids, tessellation, clustering Genetics, Evolution as a way of modelling change and fitness Emergence Perceptrons, Learning * Themes for Projects/Problems: Systems and Rules: Emergence and Complexity Learning and Adaptation Critiques: Values, Limits, Issues of validation and verification

title: Design Management and Professional Practice
description: • Designer attributes. Setting up a design office. Finding clients. • Business correspondence. Brief and briefing. Letter of contract. • Professionalism and Ethics. Costing design and fee estimation. • Management of Design Process, Human factor in managing design / team work. • Design as a Management Tool. Design Evaluation. • Patent and Design Registration laws / procedure. • Seminar on a topic related to above topics.

title: Designing Games for Children
description: • Definition of games. Differences between toys, puzzles and games. Classification of games. Learning through games. Educational games with focus on fun vs/ and education. Use of luck vs development of strategic thinking and problem solving in game design. Concepts of play value and game play. • Game design process. Iterative cycles in game design process. Player’s involvement in design process. Feedback as a source of creativity in game design. Differences between game design and other design areas. • Designing conventional game hardware and board games. Paper prototyping and testing. Play testing of games for feedback. Market for board games in India. • The elective expects developing and play-testing an original game.

title: Prodcut Semantics
description: The course discusses semantic and structural aspects of visual input, through series of project assignments. Using human information processing capabilities, the course looks back towards formal concepts such as visual complexity, visual interest and recall of forms. The first part covers topics such as spatio – temporal relationships in visual elements and its role in visual structure, forms of visual relationships, and its role in controlling visual complexity, interest and identity. Assignments are also based on application of Information theory to visual information. The second part discusses the role of characteristic features in object/form recognition and natural categorization process

title: On Ways of Seeing
description: • This course explores the inter-relationship between epistemology and ontology, between the things that we are aware of and how that shapes the way in which we conceive our world. • We will look at a series of iconic objects, including paintings, sculptures, music and films, to unravel the skeins of cultural assumptions that accompany the reception of well-known objects from the moment of their creation and through the history of their lives. • We will observe through formal analysis, examine through multiple narratives that form the discourse around such objects, especially through changing times and contexts, and analyze the themes of representation and intention that journey alongside, for a more nuanced way of looking.

title: Mobility Systems and Technology
description: Objective :* To understand the working of automobiles from a system-level approach through hands-on/field experience. * To observe common technical issues in the selected category of vehicle. * To get familiar with the engineering and technology side of automobiles and develop a broader system level understanding of mobility and vehicle technology * To learn about the developments in various aspects of technology that led to the evolution of mobility and vehicle design ** Course Contents :Some aspects that will be covered in the course include: * Exposures to systems in a vehicle-power system: Gasoline, diesel, bio-diesel, electrical, hybrids, etc * Transmission system clutch, gear trains, differential Suspension, steering, breaks, etc. * Controls: Clutch, gear, accelerator * Dashboard displays. HVAC * Vehicle structure On Frames, monocoque. * Modern assembly (robotic) and finishing technologies * Packaging & Integrating Methodology, aerodynamics, ergonomics, Seating Systems, Trim Systems, Trim Systems, Safety & Homologation * Reading and seminars. * Visit to auto plants and use of interactive software. ** Methodology and Deliverables : * Develop a morphological system map of mobility vehicles by using gigamapping techniques. * Select one of the following mobility systems: Bicycles, Motorcycles, Three-wheelers, Four-wheelers, and Commercial vehicles, and work for a week with a workshop specializing in one category. * Complete literature review of the evolution of the previously selected sub-system. Make a poster (or presentation) highlighting key observations during the workshop and the literature review. * Use modular playing devices to simulate the working principle of an automotive sub-system.

title: Quantitative Design Research Methods
description: Domains of research in quantitative methods (user behavioural research, product evaluation, hypothesis testing etc.),Scales, distributions, variables, and sampling,Statistical inference and hypothesis testing, Goodness of fit tests (z test, t test, KS test),Comparing parametric and non-parametric variables (z test, t test, Chi squared test),Analysis of variance (one way anova, two-wayanova, mixed anova),Correlation, regression, multiple regression,Factor analysis, cluster analysis,An introduction to statistical tools (MS Excel, SPSS, SAS),

description: Philosophy and historical perspectives in researchRole of objectivity and validation in research,Epistemology,Positivism, post-positivism, and scepticism,Deduction and induction,The crisis of representation,Postmodern and post-experimental inquiry,Postcolonial concerns and interpretation,Design researchWhat is design research,How is design research different from design,How is design research different from other forms of research,Why design research,Research processReviewing prior work, referencing and contextualising,Formulating a research question,Framing the hypothesis,Review of literature,Research design,Methodological framework,Data collection,Analysis,Model building,Validation / triangulation,Articulation and argument,Qualitative design research methods and toolsProtocol analysis,Semiotics and structuralism,Grounded theory,Contextual inquiry,Ethnography / visual ethnography, Case study method,Content analysis,Narrative analysis,Visual research methods,Material culture,Coding and data analysis using software (computer assisted qualitative data analysis tools e.g. Atlas.ti,MAXQDA, Excel, NVivo etc.)Design research domains that use qualitative researchUser studies,Product evaluation,Typography,Architecture,Innovation,Design pedagogy,Film Studies,Image-making and representation

title: Self-Initiated Summer Project
description: The scope of this summer project is to identify and locate, document and analyse a design resource chosen by the student. The documentation needs to be done methodically with appropriate analysis so as to bring the essence of the chosen resource. The resource could be examples of artifacts, arts, crafts, architecture, design, etc. The student is required to make both a presentation of the resource as well as do a write-up about the resource in form of a report.

title: Summer Project
description: The student takes up a summer project in order to explore an area of social concern. The student could choose a subject based on problems on any of these issues - global problems like water, food, warming; health related issues of HIV, Malaria, TB; Urban-Rural Problems, Migration, Craft and tradition, to those concerning the marginalized and people with special needs. The student should work on this problem as a design project in a design firm or with an NGO or in a research Institution.

title: Collaborative Design Project
description: . Inter-disciplinary team effort - The learning is on working collaboratively in groups to solve design problems. . Group enquiry, ideation and brainstorming . Creating project spaces and environments to facilitate innovation . User participatory design process - Iterative designing with user feedback . The project will encourage collaboration with students from other specializations, disciplines or institutes or with professionals from the industry.

title: System Design Project
description: . Design of system level solutions so that design can be thought of modularly as suited for different combinations and applications. . Understanding, strategizing, conceptualising and designing for complex systems. . Designing complex artifacts . Design solutions that are suitable for transportation, education, publishing, retailing, etc.

title: Summer Project (Focus on Industry Experience)
description: The student takes up a summer project suitable to pursue his own area of interest. This project is to help him get a hands on experience in working on a live project. The student should work on this problem as a live design project in a industry / in a design firm / with an NGO / in an innovation lab / in a research Institution.

title: Design Research Seminar I
description: . The research seminar involves student researching in an area related to design and is expected to produce new insights on the topic. . This will involve in-depth analysis of an area that is of interest to design. . The work may involve primary and secondary research, framing research questions, creative exploration of alternatives, choosing a suitable methodology for analysis, experimental set-ups and methodical documentation. The students are encouraged to make use of research methods for documentation, analysis and experimentation. . Use of photography, sketching, audio, video for research documentation purposes. The documented material is analyzed for inferences and insights. . Students are expected to explore new fields, materi­als and media, with a focus on analy­sis. . Students need to choose a topic in consultation with a faculty member and work under faculty guidance.

title: BDes Design Project - 1
description: An independent student project based on student inclination and interest. . This project allows students to identify a problem to solve and then address different issues pertaining to various segments of society under different contexts and environments.. The project also encourages students to adopt appropriate design process and methods to solve the chosen problem.

title: BDes Design Project - 2
description: The project could be any of the following: a. Design project of student interest and / or faculty interest and / or industry project b. Re-design project that re-looks at an existing problem or situation c. Research project, delving into methodological or pedagogic issues d. Exploration project, exploring application possibilities in a new technology or medium or variations. This project would involve all aspects of the design process starting from problem identification to complete solution. The student is expected to make use of the knowledge and skills gained during the previous years to independently handle a design project. . The project can be multi-disciplinary in nature involving many of these disciplines 302226 Product Design, Interaction Design, Communication Design, Animation, etc.. This project can also be sponsored by an industry or other organizations.

title: M.Des. Project-1
description: The summer internship provides an opportunity for you to work in rural or urban scenarios, based on your preferences and it hopes let you explore the impact design can have on our society, given the backdrop of socio-economic and cultural context of our country. The duration of the project is four weeks. All the students will have to make a presentation after they have completed their internships.

title: M.Des. Project- 2
description: • Duration: 4 months from July– Nov. • This project will be an extension of the project III and should include development of the final design concept. The defence presentations will be held during the month of April and time given till the end of June to complete the jury feedback, final drawings and finer detailing of the project. • The evaluation of the Stage 2 of Project III is done by a panel of examiners appointed by DPGC. The panel will consist of external jury member along with an internal examiner, the guide and the chairman

title: Basic Epidemiology
description: Definition and objectives of epidemiology: Principles and methods of epidemiology to investigate disease distribution. Using epidemiologic methods to study the cause, extent and prevention of various infectious and non-infectious diseases. Dynamics of disease transmission: modes of transmission, attack rate, disease outbreak investigation  Disease surveillance and measures of morbidity and mortality: Active and passive surveillance, emergency warning systems, stages of a disease, incidence and prevalence of disease, mortality rates, case fatality Assessing screening tests: Validity of tests, Tests with binary and continuous outcomes, sequential testing, sensitivity and specificity measures Different types of study design, including randomized trials, case-control and cohort studies, risk estimation and causal inferences. Epidemiology and the development of policy.

title: Introduction to Public Health Informatics
description: Introduction to public health with emphasis on health promotion and disease prevention. History of public health work, Role of government in health, public health policies and interventions Introduction to global health, health inequities, Epidemic and pandemic preparedness, Environmental issues in public health. Mapping and surveillance techniques for controlling diseases, models for efficient and knowledge based public health work Introduction to biomedical informatics and public health informatics, role of informatics in public health and global health Standards in Biomedical and public health Informatics Evaluation of Biomedical and Health Information Resources, Patient-Centered Care Systems, Patient Monitoring Systems, Clinical Research Informatics Introduction to ethics in healthcare and its importance. Issues in Clinical ethics and Research ethics. Studying application of healthcare technology and ethical issues. Main principles of medical ethics Overview of the Clinical Research Process. WHO Principles of Good Clinical Practice. Ethical Conduct Good Clinical Practice. Protocol. Review by Independent Ethics Committee/ Independent Review Board. Protocol Compliance. Informed Consent Roles and responsibilities of Principal. Investigator Qualifications. Staff Qualifications. Records. Confidentiality and Data Privacy

Health Insurance Demand for Health Insurance and moral hazard Health insurance markets and adverse selection Public Health Insurance Programs Health Service Disparities 4. Market for Physicians 5. Economics of Hospitals 6. Health Behaviours: substance abuse, smoking, obesity 7. Burden of Illness Communicable diseases Non-communicable diseases 8. Health Policy Evaluation
