CO and higher paraffins oxidation; Methane combustion; Chemical time scales 1.4 Review of transport phenomena: Overview of heat and mass transfer; Conservation of mass and species; Conservation of momentum and conservation of energy 1.5 Ignition Phenomena: Autoignition based on thermal theory; Effect of pressure on auto- ignition temperature; Piloted ignition; Condensed fuel ignition 1.6 Laminar Premixed Flames: Physical processes in a premixed flame; Flammability limits; Flame quenching; Minimum energy for sustained ignition and flame propagation; Factors influencing flame velocity and thickness; Flame speed correlations for selected fuels 1.7 Laminar Diffusion Flames: Description of a candle flame; Laminar jet flame height; Condensed fuel fires 1.8 Applications of turbulent premixed and diffusion combustion a) Spark ignition (SI) engine combustion b) Compression ignition (CI) engine combustion 1.9 Droplet Evaporation and Combustion: Droplet vaporization in quiescent air; Droplet combustion; Initial heating of a droplet; Droplet distribution 1.10 Emissions: Negative effects of combustion products; Pollutant formation; Quantification of emissions; Emissions from premixed combustion; Emissions from diffusion combustion.

title: Introduction to Particulate Flow
description: Introduction to particulate matter: Definition, occurrence and examples of granular and particulate flow Basics: particle characterisation, size distribution and shape, inter-particle interactions, collisions, deformation, friction, angle of repose, Janssen effect, flow regimes Theory of slow flow: Mohr-Coulomb yield condition, flow rules and co-axiality condition modelling of flow through hoppers Theory of Rapid flow: kinetic theory of granular materials, analysis of flow in simple geometries, Hybrid theory: example of chute flow Modelling of Solid-Fluid Mixture: continuum and discrete approach through example of fluidised beds

title: Multiphase Petroleum Transportation
description:  Fundamentals of Single Phase Flow: Introduction of mass, momentum and energy bal- ance for single phase flow. Introduction of frictional factor in Laminar and turbulent single phase pipe flow.  Fundamentals of Multiphase Flow: Introduction to principles of multiphase flow, differ- ent flow regimes, pressure and temperature prediction in the pipeline and methodology for pipeline sizing.  Phase Behaviour and Physical Properties of different oil: Analysis of physical proper- ties for oil recovered from different climatic region and prediction of their compositional phase behaviour and PVT prediction software.  Steady State Multiphase flow: Introduction to steady state multiphase flow, wells, flow- lines and risers. Pipeline design for fluid consisting oil/gas and gas. Design for slugging flows. Solids transportation. Design for heavy oil transport. Introduction of available soft- ware for reservoirs design.  Design of Offshore and Onshore Pipelines: Pipeline sizing, installation, thermal insula- tion and corrosion protection.  Subsea transportation in oil industries: Subsea production system architecture.  Transient Multiphase Flow: Management of pipeline inventory and sizing of slug catcher. Their integration with process plant. Transient thermal analysis.

title: Energy Policy Analysis
description: The course will provide the tools and techniques necessary for analyzing energy policies. Students will be expected to carry out a project to analyse an existing policy in a specific context or design a policy intervention for a specific goal. Overview of energy policy instruments – pricing, regulation, Incentives, subsidies Framework for policy analysis, stakeholders, criteria – energy access, security, sustainability, development Case studies of a few energy policies – successes and failures, Free riders and rebound effect Reference Energy system, End Use Analysis Modelling Energy Demand, Elasticities Susbtitution- S-shaped Logistic curves, Examples of accelerated diffusion- factors affecting diffusion Economy wide impacts- Input-Output models, Optimisation models, TIMES-MARKAL Scenario generation – approach and examples Energy policy analysis project

title: Energy Laboratory III
description: Performance of pin fin, Electrochemical system I/V characteristics of fuel cell, Performance of heat exchanger, Characterization of solar cells (I/V curve, structural response, effect of temperature on different types of cells), Characterisation of PV modules (I / V curve under series/parallel connection), Determination of drift and drag coefficient of turbine blades, Characterize and model leakage through labrynth seal, Compressible flow through converging and diverging ducts, Fuel Cell demonstration experiment, Gasifier testing, IC engine testing, Performance of converging and diverging nozzle.

title: IC Engine, Alternate Fuels and Emissions
description: Introduction: IC engines classifications (SI & CI engine, 2-stroke & 4-stroke engine), Thermodynamic Cycles Combustion in IC engine: Thermochemistry of Fuel-Air mixture, characterization of flame, Combustion stoichiometry, Chemical equilibrium, Chemical kinetics Properties of fuel and its effect on combustion: Engine knock & detonation, abnormal combustion Engine Emissions and Control: Theory of NO x , SO x , CO, UHC & PM formation, Emission norms, role of fuel, lubricant, engine design and operation parameters on emissions Alternate fuels, properties, suitability and emissions: CNG, LPG, H 2 , Hythane, Di-Methyl Ether, Ethanol, Biodiesel Novel technologies and strategies to curb emissions: Homogeneous charge CI (HCCI) engines, Premixed Charge Compression Ignition (PCCI), Emission control technologies (EGR, SCR, DOC, DPF etc) (To be updated periodically with new technologies and strategies) Hands on exposure (in lab) on Petrol & Diesel engine, and fuel properties study

title: Electrochemical Energy Storage
description: 1. Thermodynamics and Kinetics of Electrochemical Reactoions2. Introduction to Electrochemical Techniques:(a) Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) and its applications(b) Cycling Voltammetry and Linear Polarization (c) Galvanostatic Intermittent Titration3. Electrochemical Energy Storage Systems(a) Advanced Rechargeable Batteries(b) Supercapacitors4. Hybrid power systems: Differences/interactions between batteries and supercapacitors5. Current distribution in electrochemical Systems6. GFluid flow and mass transfer problems in Electrochemical systems7. Porous electrode theory8. Modelling and Simulation of Batteris and Supercapacitors.

title: Energy Efficient Industrial Drives
description: Overview: Variable speed AC motor drives are widely used in industrial applications and power electronic converters (AC-DC and DC-AC) are essential elements of these industrial drives. This course aims at providing an insight into the various energy efficient AC motors, power electronic converters and the associated filters. Design, control and modulation strategies of the power electronic converters to achieve superior power conversion efficiency, waveform quality, dynamic response and durability are also discussed. Course Syllabus: Module 1 - Introduction: Energy flow in an industrial drive, low voltage (LV) and medium voltage (MV) drives, popular drive topologies - types of motors and power converters used, factors affecting the efficiency, losses in different sub-systems of an industrial drive Module 2 - Line-side converters and the corresponding control methods: 6-pulse diode and thyristor rectifiers, harmonic distortion at the point of common coupling, 12-pulse and 24-pulse diode/thyristor rectifiers, PWM rectifiers and their controller structures, phase-locked-loop (PLL), Design of grid-side filter (L, LC and LCL) and DC-link inductor and/or capacitor, control of reactive power compensator and harmonic compensator Module 3 - Pulse width modulation techniques and their influences: Carrier-based and space-vector-based approaches to PWM, PWM methods for 2-level and 3-level voltage source inverters (VSIs): sine-triangle PWM, third harmonic injection PWM, conventional space vector PWM, bus-clamping/discontinuous PWM, influence of pulse width modulation (PWM) method on the AC-side harmonic distortion, converter losses and torque ripple Module 4 - Motor-side converters and the corresponding control methods for induction motors: Field-oriented (vector) control of 3-phase squirrel-cage induction motor (SCIM), effects of variation in machine parameters on vector control, methods of parameter adaptation, sensor-less vector control of VSI-fed SCIM drive, direct torque control, fixed switching frequency operation in direct torque control, operation of the drive in field-weakening mode, self-commissioning of induction motor drives Module 5 - Motor-side converters and the corresponding control methods for permanent magnet synchronous motors: Vector control of 3-phase permanent magnet synchronous motor (PMSM), shaft encoders and estimation of rotor position, maximum torque per ampere (MTPA) control, zero d-axis current (ZDAC) control, operation of the drive in field-weakening mode Module 6 - Motor-side filters: Design of output LC-filter (sine filter) for a VSI-fed drive, vector control of SCIM-drive with LC filter, parasitic elements in a variable frequency drive, grounding and cabling of AC drive systems, common-mode equivalent circuit, effects of long motor cables - voltage doubling, design of dv/dt filters and common-mode filters for VSI-fed drives

title: Diffraction-based Characterization of Energy Materials
description: 1. Introduction: (A) Energy materials: Defect-decorated materials, Layered-materials, Functional-oxides, Heterostructures, Low-dimensional materials (B) Structures: Long & short range order and amorphous; Pair Distribution Functions (PDFs) 2. Diffraction & Scattering: Reciprocal lattice; Ewald Sphere (A) Diffraction: Laue’s equations, Bragg’s law – Treatment in real and reciprocal spaces; Bloch waves (B) Scattering: Elastic scattering, Atomic scattering factor, Structure factor, Back-scatter and characteristic signals, Inelastic scattering 3. Hardware: X ray and electron sources; Lenses and Apertures – Condenser, Objective and Diffraction; Resolution; Spherical and Chromatic Aberrations; Astigmatism; Detectors 4. Imaging: (A) Scanning Electron Microscopy: Secondary, Back-Scatter; Magnification, Focus, Astigmation, Depth of field; Interaction volume a. CaSiNO 3.3: Hands-on training b. Virtual SEM: Hands-on training (B) Transmission Electron Microscopy: Bright and Dark field imaging, STEM imaging a. Virtual TEM: Hands-on training 5. Diffraction Patterns: Selection rules; X ray diffraction patterns; Selected Area Electron Diffraction Patterns (SAEDPs); g-vector; Intensity; Indexing: Single (SC, FCC, BCC and HCP) and polycrystals; Double and dynamic Diffraction a. PowderCell 2.3: Hands-on training b. Various Software and Online resources for indexing unknown SAEDPs: Hands-on training on single and multiple phase Energy Materials

title: Micro and Nanoscale Energy Transport
description: ontinuum laws and limitations ; Energy carriers at micro/nanoscale and their attributes; Engineering applications, Size effects 2. Energy states Schrodinger equation; Energy states of quantum wells, harmonic oscillators, rigid rotors, hydrogen atoms, electrons and phonons in crystals; Applicability in nanoscale devices such as quantum dot lasers, superlattices and photonic crystals 3. Energy storage Density of states; Ensembles; molecular partition functions; specific heat of gases; Fermi-Dirac and Bose Einstein distributions; Specific heat of phonons and electrons 4. Energy transport Energy transport by waves: plane electron waves and reflection, electromagnetic waves and reflection at single interface, interference and tunneling, acoustic waves; Energy transport by particles: Wave to particle transition, Boltzmann equation, scattering mechanisms and relaxation time approximation, Derivation of continuum laws including Fourier law, Newton’s shear stress law, Ohm’s law from the Boltzmann equation Transport by liquid: Derivation of Navier Stokes equation for gas molecules, Single phase liquid flow and heat transport in microtubes and channels; Two-phase heat transfer in microchannels; 5. Energy conversion: Nonequilibrium between different heat carriers, nonequilibrium between electrons and phonons in laser-materials interaction, Hot and cold electron effects in semiconductor devices and thermoelectric devices 6. Simulation and measurement techniques in micro and nanoscale transport processes Introduction to Monte Carlo technique and molecular dynamics ; Scanning thermal microscopy; Optical techniques for local measurements and short-time heat transfer effects

title: Modeling and Control of Inverter-based Renewable Resources
description: Electrical modeling and Design-oriented Analysis of solar PV, batteries & supercapacitors; Inverter modeling approaches--- frame transformations, dynamic phasors, State-Space and Frequency-Domain modeling for control; PLLs for grid synchronization; Grid- Following (GFL) control of inverters, anti-islanding schemes, next-generation control paradigms---Grid-Forming (GFM) of inverters---droop, VSM, VOC flavors; Stability analysis, fault ride through capabilities; Grid codes and standards 1547 & 2800; Common-mode modeling of inverter circuits, electromagnetic noise, filter design for EMI/EMC.

title: Electric Vehicle Grid Integration
description: Introduction: History of Electric Vehicles (EV), EV charging technology and its grid integration; introduction to early developments of EV integration in EV rich countries Electric Vehicle Charging Infrastructure: EV charging technology; EVSE for 2W/3W/4W/Heavy duty vehicles (E-Trucks; E-buses); EV charging stations; EV charging standards, communication protocols; smart charging for EVs Regulations for Electric Vehicle Charging: EV charging regulations; EV tariff structure; grid connection regulations for EV charging stations EV Integration with Distribution System: Fleet aggregation; Scheduling of EV load; impact of EV integration on voltage profile, feeder loading, losses, and other power quality issues; congestion management; unbalanced system under EV demand; influence on reactive power exchange between distribution and transmission system; impact on distribution system protection EV Integration with Transmission System: Impact of EV integration on system demand; impact on frequency and voltage stability; inertial and primary frequency support from EVs, reactive power and voltage control support from EVs; estimation of reserves from EVs; scheduling of EV load; other ancillary services from EVs Electric Vehicle and Renewable Energy Integration: RE based EV charging; coordinated operation of EVs and distributed generation; captive RE plants for EV charging; RE integrated EVs; facilitation of increased RE penetration at system level through EV charging

title: Power Converters for Electric Vehicle Charging
description: Introduction to EV charging-levels and modes of charging, on-board and off-board charging; Design, modelling and control of Power factor correction (PFC) converters- Active front-end, Totem pole PFC, Bridgeless interleaved boost, Vienna PFC; Battery chargers (DC-DC) - Two switch forward, Phase-shifted full bridge, Dual active bridge (single, dual and triple phase-shift modulation), LLC and LCC resonant converters, ZCS/ZVS; Integrated on-board chargers - Machine (IM, PMSM, SRM) integrated on-board chargers, ensuring zero torque in 3-ph topologies, motor winding re-configuration, split winding machines.

splitting and multi application High efficiency c-Si solar cellslosses in solar cells; optical, electrical, ways to overcome the losses, concept of selective emitter, N-type and P-type cells, CIGS)Electrical and optical properties of materials solar cell structure, principle of operation, material deposition techniques, cell performanceMulti-junction III_V solar cellsIntroduction to III-V description: Overview of world energy scenario, Energy Crisis, Fossil Fuel reserves , India`s energy scenario,Primary energy analysis, Environmental Impacts. Biomass Energy conversion, Wind energy conversion, Fuell Cells and other energy conversion technologies. Economic viability of alternatives, Sustainability.

description: The following is a list of topics of which a majority will be covered for all students (in any given year) Week # Topics Innovation 1 Importance of innovation, design thinking and creative problem-solving 2 Design principles; System design, embodiment, and components 3 Design brief; Concept generation; Idea sketching 4 Proof-of-concept fabrication; Rapid prototyping 5 Prototype testing; Early customer feedback; Design improvement 6 Design for manufacturability; design for environment and other factors 7 Intellectual property rights; Technology licensing models. title: Introduction to Entrepreneurship
description: Course Description: Now more than ever is a great time to create value and to create wealth in emerging economies as an entrepreneur. This activity-oriented foundation course provides the necessary context and content for learners interested in innovation and entrepreneurship. Learners will gain an understanding of the Entrepreneurial process – the entrepreneurial mindset, opportunity identification and innovation, customer discovery and Go-To-Market, entrepreneurial finance and technology venture creation. This course will use a combination of concept teaching and real-life storytelling to bring alive the entrepreneurial journey for learners. Learners can apply the principles and practices taught through a hands-on course project. There have been ventures created by students who have taken this course, and some of them will be covered as case studies. Course instruction is by a faculty team who bring in experience and expertise in entrepreneurship, academia, industry. Learning Objectives: (1) Getting into the entrepreneurial mindset (2) Gaining an understanding of the innovation and entrepreneurship process (3) Recognizing opportunities for new business (4) Getting hands-on during the Customer Discovery Process (5) Understanding Technology evolution and product and business life-cycles (6) Gaining insights into startup financing (7) Understanding of business functions essential for success of technology enterprises. Course Pre-requisite: None. B.Tech 2nd - 4th Year & M.Tech Students, PhD and Post-Doctoral Researchers can enroll. Course Evaluation: Mid Sem & End Sem (50%), Groups of 4-6 Project Business Model Canvas( 30%), Attendance. Class Participation , Assignments (20%)

description:  Evolution of marketing management  Role of Marketing within the Startup Framework  Problem identification for Starting Up  Quick & rough Market Research  Customer Segmentation and Focus for Launch  Customer Value proposition & Accelerated Product/Solution design  Lean Marketing Strategy for New Ventures  Early Stage Positioning, Branding & Communicating  New Introductions : Pricing and Commercial Strategy  Managing Marketing Spend and Measuring ROI  Test marketing a New Product / solution  Competitive Defense and Differentiation  Scaling Up Profitably

Customer Value Proposition, Rapid prototyping & Validation 3. Customer Branding, Lean advertising; Pricing; Go-to-Market & After-Market Plan, Supply Chain,Customer Channels 4. Sales strategy, skills & tools, Customer Relationship Management 5. Accounting: Assets, Liabilities, Revenue, Costs, Cash Flows etc. 6. Financial Statements: Balance sheet, Income - Cash flow - Equity statement, etc. 9. Corporate Finance: Objective of a firm; Time value of money; Financial Decision making 10. Startup Funding: Bootstrapping, Grants, Incubators & Accelerators, Angels, PE/VC, IPO Team Assignments : I. Business Plans II. Investor Pitch

title: Managing Innovation and IP for Entrepreneurs
description: Content(List of the topics/sub-topics to be covered in the lectures/practicals/assignments): A. Technology-led Innovation 1.Introduction: Innovation through the Ages; Change as an opportunity; Types of Change (technology, demographic, social, economic, etc.)2.Technology lifecycle: Technology trajectory and drivers; technology lifecycle; S-curve; Use-cases3.Analysis of Innovation: Types (breakthrough/incremental); Iterative thinking; Relationship to IPR4.Opportunity Identification: Pain-Wave-Waste; Bright spots; Quick assessment; Progress feasibilityB. Problem, Solution and Venture 5.Challenge framing: Min-Max framing; Metaphoric thinking; Challenge validation6.Problem solving approaches: Inventive/Design Thinking; Creativity tools (TRIZ, Bionics, etc.) 7.Solution validation: Prototyping and experimentation; Customer feedback; Empathy and re-framing; Hypothesis thinking8.Business model exploration: B-model templates; Who-What-How Model; Value capture through IPRC. Patents and Other Intellectual Property 9.Fundamentals of IP: Role and value; Different forms of IP rights; IIT Bombay Policies for IP and licensing 10.Patent Basics: Patentability criteria and exceptions; Types of patents; Patent systems around the world; Applications including software and business methods 11.Patent Analytics: How to read a patent; Patent databases; Search and analytical tools 12.Role in R&D and Business Planning: Patent landscaping; Competitive intelligenceandsurveillance; New venture evaluation; Techno-legal studies (ex. FTO)13.Other IPR: Copyright, Trade Marks, Service Marks, Designsand other forms of IP), Trade SecretsD. Commercialization and Management of IP 14.Patents to Profits: Techno-commercial evaluation; IP due diligence; IP strategy and IP models; IP value capturing opportunities15.IP Asset Management: Patent portfolio; IP Licensing/Acquisition, IP Enforcement and prosecution16.Innovation Culture: Promoting creativityand handling failures; Scenario planning; Technology road mapping; IP value hierarchy(defensive to visionary).

title: Introduction to Business Models
description: The course is envisaged in 3 phases. The first phase of the course will focus on the theory of business models and why they matter. It will discuss the components and elements of a business model. This phase will lay the framework for business models and drive the linkage between technological change / disruption and new business models. In the second phase, we will cover references and case studies of different business models across industries and business types. These would include manufacturing, services, platform business models, Digital, Informal Economy, Inclusive / Circular Economy / Fair Trade, and Social Business Models. In the third and final phase of the course, we will discuss how continuous innovation in business models can be managed. Students / student teams will be presented with business model challenges (successes / failures) for working upon as course projects.

title: Field Immersion for Researchers and Entrepreneurs
description: 302225 Ethnography as a methodology for Innovation & entrepreneurship practice and research 302225 Gaining access to the field and ethics of ethnographic inquiry 302225 Participant and Non participant observations 302225 Forms of data - Visual data, historical data, videos and articles 302225 Data Collection - conducting Ethnographic interviews, shadowing the protagonists, artefacts 302225 Data Storage and organization 302225 Field notes and thick description 302225 Open and Axial Coding

title: Social Enterprises & Inclusive Business
description: Social Entrepreneurship Concept, Ideas & Models: Introduction to the course + Setting the Context: Social entrepreneurship, Inclusive Business & Social Impact, theoretical underpinnings, agriculture + education, financial services + healthcare, Technology-based / digital solutions, Understanding low-income customers/ consumers + Inclusive Markets; Inclusive Business Concepts: Grassroots Innovation & Entrepreneurs, Business at the Base of the Pyramid (BoP);Conventional Business & Social Impact: Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR), Environmental, Social & Governance (ESG) practices

title: Business to Business (B2B) Sales and Marketing
description: • Introduction to B2B Sales & Marketing • Understanding the B2B Buyer & Decision-Making Unit (DMU) • B2B Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) • Creating the Value Proposition, Messaging & Pitch • B2B Marketing & Outreach Strategy • Leveraging Digital Marketing in B2B • B2B Sales Strategies and Models • B2B Pricing Strategies • B2B Procurement: Definitions, Stakeholders and Processes • Sales and Marketing Organisation Alignment • Building and Maintaining Client Relationships • B2B SaaS Marketing & Sales Strategies • Understanding the MarTech landscape for B2B

title: Strategy and Leadership for Entrepreneurs
description: Modules : Basics of Strategic Thinking - Why bother about strategy. - Definition and components of strategy - Strategic thinking vs. operational thinking Choosing where to play - Why are you starting this business? What is your market insight? -What is your advantage (first mover, access to scarce talent, IP, brand, ecosystem leverage) - Framework for choices involved in where to play. Establishing your Right to Win / Positioning and Differentiation - Why should the customer choose you - Creating a unique value proposition - Why are you better than the competition from a customer’s perspective - Why are you better than the competition from an investor’s perspective Operating Strategies -- Capabilities & Systems Financial Model - Growth, Profitability, and Cash / Assessing business viability and attractiveness - Creating value : what comprises true unit economics, how can scalability be achieved with robust economics, (is there a) distinction between valuation and value; how to assess impact from multiple stakeholder lenses, trade-offs if any - Network Effect – Speed to Scale as a strategic lever - Operating Leverage: Unit economics Vs Investment - Sustainable Growth – Managing Cash and RoE - Risk vs Return – Founder Vs VC/PE risk appetite Leadership - Qualities of an entrepreneur: risk taker, about Co-founders, team builder, external networker, personal resilience - Traits of effective leaders - Leadership Styles and Theories - Recruiting and keeping high performers - Conflict resolution - Managing in uncertainty / Conviction, Agility, and Resilience - Planning for uncertainty: types of uncertainty and responses to them - Monitoring and adjusting strategy -- When and how to hold the line, pivot, adapt

Social entrepreneurship 6. Informal Economy and entrepreneurship 7. Poverty, exclusion and entrepreneurship 8. Necessity Entrepreneurship 9. Innovation at the Bottom / Base of the Pyramid 10. ICT4Good / Tech for Development 11. Digitisation an inclusion / exclusion 12. Jugaad and bricolage 13. Frugal Innovation 14. Innovation and entrepreneurship policy for societal good

title: Tech-Opportunities Identification
description: Recognizing Entrepreneurial Opportunities and Generating Ideas Types of Change (Technology, social and demographic factors, Economy and Industry factors) Transforming science into Business Technology, Industry and Competitor Analysis Feasibility Analysis Developing an Effective Business Model

title: Entrepreneurial Financing and Venture Capital
description: Many of India`s most successful entrepreneurial companies have been substantially influenced by Angels in the early stage and professionally managed venture capital subsequently for growth. This relationship is examined from both the entrepreneur`s and the venture capitalist`s perspective. From the point of view of the entrepreneur, the course considers how significant business opportunities are identified, planned, and built into sustainable companies; how resources are matched with opportunity; and how, within this framework, founders seek capital and partner with Angels / venture capitalists for value creation . From the point of view of the venture capitalist, the course considers how potential entrepreneurial investments are evaluated, valued, structured, and incentivised for; how different venture capital strategies are deployed; and how venture capitalists raise and manage their own funds. The course includes a term-long project where students work in teams (4-5 students per team) to assess and structure a multi stage funding plan that meets both founder and Venture Capitalists goals.

title: AI for Business and Entrepreneurs
description: This course offers an introduction to the 1) impact of AI on startups and businesses, and 2) the emergence of new AI startups. Students will learn how AI is useful for businesses, and analyse the kind of functions and areas where AI may be applied. Students will learn how AI driven data feed into decision-making and more efficient processes. The course will also discuss how to think about the impact of AI implementation, and what leadership means in a rapidly changing AI-driven scenario. Students will learn about the pitfalls of AI including working with low-quality data, and ethical implications of Gen AI. In this course we will cover the following aspects: 1. Introduction to Gen AI and the AI landscape and why it is important for businesses to look at and understand AI. 2. Examples and use cases of how AI is applied in different business functions and sectors. How AI tools can be used in common business domains, functions and processes, how AI-driven data feeds into decision-making. We look at AI implementation examples in different business functions. 3. New business opportunities emerging out of AI and emerging AI first business. 4. Impact of AI on industries and sectors 5. Responsible AI: Ethics, Bias, Regulation 6. Role of leadership and change management: how to lead and manage AI-driven change.

title: Entrepreneuring by Leveraging University Ecosystems
description: • You, your professors and entrepreneurship opportunities : Based on personal interest, identifying and visiting a total of 5 labs in atleast two different departments to understand ongoing technology development through interaction with respective professors and doctoral students Identify atleast one student who has co-founded and registered a venture, and conduct interview based on discussion guide developed for the purpose. [Week 1 -2] • Getting out of the building - exploring the campus and nearby areas for resources Choose a context for investigation in or around campus, visit the site to identify stakeholders. Conduct interviews with various stakeholders Document potential opportunities for oneself and make a related presentation Identify and visit a shop for raw material and fabrication site which can be leveraged for prototyping [Week 3 -4] • Seeking out team members and contributors in the local ecosystem Identify and meet atleast one startup team which comprises collegemates. Interview based on discussion guide developed for the purpose and record insights. Prepare ‘Startup Founder’ profile of oneself based on framework discussed in classroom Prepare a co-founder profile Identify and interview atleast 3 potential co-founders among student community at IITB. Document the interviews, reflect on findings and make a presentation on insights [Week 5] • Events and competitions, classrooms, courses and product development Identify one competition / hackathon which you could participate in considering the opportunity that has been identified in Sl No 2. Prepare submission document / video as required by the competition Identify a set of courses (credit courses, online courses, coursera etc) and make a presentation explaining their appropriateness for your development as an entrepreneur. Identify resources for product development on campus including student clubs, labs and make a presentation explaining how these resources can be leveraged for actual development of your product prototype [Week 6] • Securing funds – institute, alumni, incubation centres, grants Study the journey of atleast 3 startup teams involving students and through interviews, document their funding journey. Make a presentation with insights gathered thorugh this process Identify funding sources which could be most relevant for your idea and make a presentation on the same [Week 7]

title: Fintech Entrepreneurship
description: FinTech is transforming how we access, use, and think about financial services. From digital payments and lending platforms to AI-driven investment tools and blockchain innovations, technology is creating new possibilities for disruption across the financial sector.This course offers a practical and accessible introduction to the world of FinTech, ideal for aspiring entrepreneurs, professionals, and curious learners alike. Through conceptual frameworks, real-world use cases, and case studies, participants will explore how FinTech is reshaping banking, lending, and financial services, and understand its broader impact at both business and national levels.The Desai Sethi School of Entrepreneurship (DSSE) is the ideal home for such a course. With its focus on innovation, disruption, and venture creation, DSSE empowers students to not just understand FinTech but to build within it.Located in Mumbai, the financial capital of India, IIT Bombay offers students direct exposure to leading banks, startups, investors, and policymakers. This proximity, combined with the entrepreneurial environment at DSSE, gives students a unique opportunity to launch impactful FinTech ventures and shape the future of financial services from one of the most dynamic ecosystems in the country.Introduction to FinTech1 Understand what FinTech is and why it matters2 Explore the history and evolution of financial technologies3 Get a broad overview of the FinTech ecosystemCore Concepts Behind FinTech4 Learn how finance, technology, and economics come together5 Understand major categories and types of FinTech solutions Key Trends in FinTech1 Digital payments and banking innovation2 Crowdfunding, lending platforms, and alternative finance3 Neo-banking, investment tech, and financial disruptionRegulations and Stakeholders1 Role of central banks and regulators2 Insights from investors and industry expertsApplications and Impact1 Real-world use cases in banking and financial services2 Case studies on FinTech302222s impact at business and national levels3 Explore how FinTech is reshaping financial inclusion and efficiencyEntrepreneurship Opportunities in Fintech Sector

title: Supply Chain & Distribution Management for Technopreneurs
description: Course is designed to provide an end-to-end overview of Supply Chain & Distribution Management for Technopreneurs, with particular focus on early stage ventures and new product launches. The curriculum includes theoretical concepts, real-world case studies & assignments. Course content will span diverse industries like FMCG, Electronics, Pharma, Cold Chain, Industrial, Chemical, and Project Supply ChainsTool-kits to assess and design best-fit supply chain for new product launches will be discussed. 1.Introduction to Supply Chain & Distribution Management 2.Warehousing and Material Handling Management 3.Transportation Management, Express and Cold Chain Logistics 4.Supply Chain Planning and MRP I & II 5.Supply Chain Optimization and Big Data Analytics 6.Automation in Supply Chain and Warehousing 7.Supplier Management, In-bound & Assembly Line Logistics 8. Distribution Channels: Distributors, Retailers, and E-commerce 9.Demand Forecasting and Inventory Management 10.Customer Pricing, Order Management and Fulfillment 11.Emerging Trends and Sustainability in Supply Chain & Distribution12.Go-to-Market strategies for Entrepreneurs & New Product Launches Capstone Project: Supply Chain and Distribution Channel Design for Startups / New Product

title: Core Topics in Innovation and Entrepreneurship
description: Technology and Innovation Management:: 1. Innovation Processes : Indicators, role of digital tools, emotions 2. Firm Boundaries and Technological Innovation: Organizational structure and innovation, open innovation, collaboration networks 3. Frontiers in Innovation Research: Platforms, disruptive innovation, idea screening, innovation adoption, artificial intelligence. 4. Entrepreneurship- Landscaping the field: What is entrepreneurship? Who is (not) an entrepreneur? What is the role of the entrepreneur? What questions define the field? 5. Sources of Entrepreneurship: Creation / discovery of opportunities 6. Entrepreneurial Resources: key resources for entrepreneurs, and how entrepreneurs acquire / build them 7. Corporate Entrepreneurship: Process models, issues of legitimacy and identity 8. Entrepreneurial Support Organizations: Incubators, accelerators, science parks, maker spaces, co-working spaces) 9. Venture Financing: How are entrepreneurial ventures financed, monitored and governed? 10. Business Models: Importance, design and pivots 11. Institutional Context of Entrepreneurship: How do markets and fields get created? How does entrepreneurship unfold in emerging contexts? 12. Entrepreneurial Ecosystems: Distinct features, metrics and associated labour

description: X

title: Technology, Design and Innovation
description: 1. Technology innovation 2. Innovation opportunities 3. Research and translation 4. Problem-solving approaches 5. Product design 6. Design for X (lifecycle issues) 7. Prototyping 8. Product validation 9. Innovation Team 10. Project management 11. Intellectual property rights 12. Patents 13. Other IPR 14. Patents to profits 15. Continuous improvement

title: Business Finance for Technopreneurs
description: 1. Business landscape 2. Role of finance 3. Financial assets and liabilities 4. Capital, debt and equity 5. Product pricing 6. Breakeven analysis 7. Startup financing sources 8. Balance sheet 9. Financial ratios 10. Accounting and cash flow 11. Financial risk 12. Venture capital 13. Funding negotiations 14. Valuation and sustenance 15. Startup to scaleup

title: Marketing and Sales for Entrepreneurs
description: 1. Branding and team 2. Customer and market sizing 3. Customer persona 4. Customer value proposition 5. Minimum viable product 6. Customer validation 7. Go-to-market and after-market plan 8. Branding and advertising plan 9. Pricing plan 10. B2C distribution and channel 11. B2B customer funnel 12. Sales strategy and capability 13. Sales and customer service 14. Organization design 15. Investor value proposition

title: Technology Venture Formation
description: 1. Technology ventures 2. Startup ecosystem 3. Incubators and parks 4. Startup venture resources 5. Reality check 1 (problem) 6. Reality Check 2 (solution) 7. Go‐to‐market for startups 8. Financial projections 9. Startup team 10. Capital for startup 11. Business model 12. Proposal pitching 13. Startup incorporation 14. Social entrepreneurship 15. Business growth

title: MENG -Project
description: MENG-ENTE Project

title: Seminar
description: Ph.D. Seminar

title: Optics
description: Electromagnetic wave description of light, waves in vacuum,plane waves,polarization(stokes parameters), confined waves,Gaussian wave propagation,diffraction free beams, waves in isotropic media, optical response of media, Lorentz and Drude models, dispersion and absorption,wave propagation in uniaxial media, Reflection and refraction,Fresnel`s equations and their consequences. Interference,Fabry-Perot and Michelson interferometers, interference coatings,Spatial and temporal coherence,introduction to Fourier transforms,convolution theorem, Fourier transform spectroscopy, optical coherence tomography, Fraunhofer diffraction, diffraction gratings and their uses, Fabri-perot resonator with gain, stimulated emission,lasers and holography.

title: Introduction to Special Theory of Relativity
Physics Laboratory I
description: Velocity of sound in solids, Mesurement of magnetic induction B by search coil, e/m for electorn by ma gnetoron method, viscosity by Stoke"s method, thermal conductivity by Forbe"s method, Rating of Vacuum pump, self and mutual inductance by ballistic galvanometer, stydy of electromagnetic indution, Variation of specific heat of graphite with temperature ,linear expansion by interference method.

title: Physics Laboratory II
description: H-photoelectric effect, ultrasonic grating Frank-Hertz experiment, Millikan"s oil drop experiement, Elastic Constants by interference method (Cornu"s fringes), susceptibility by Guoy"s method, Air track-study of potential well, Faraday effect-verdet"s constant, Dielectric constant and dipole moment of molecules, particle size estimation by diffraction.

title: Electronics Laboratory I
description: Testing of electronic components and solidering practice, design of printed circuit board, conversion of galvanometer to voltmeter and ammeter, output resistanceof a D.C.source, use of cathode ray oscillcope for measuring frequency, amplitude of different wave shape, etc., study of R.C. circuit with constant and variable E.M.F.,phase measurement in R.C.circuit with low frequency A.C.,study of L.R. and LCR circuits, Q factor of LCR circuit,Measurement of Capacitance by Schering Bridge. Design of multimeter,filtering and phase shifting networks, Regulations characteristics of rectifier to obtain ripple factor in different filters, semi-conductor diode characteristics, Bi-polar transisteor characteristics,Applications of diode (clipping and a.c.voltage multiplier), Response of indicating instruting-analogue meter, digital meter,CRO, etc., Logic gate circuits I: all six gates-truth table verification, Logic gate circuits II: R-S-Flip-flop, J-K Flip-flop,etc.

title: Electronics Laboratory II
description: Design of multimeter. Filtering and phase shifting networks. Regulation characteristics of rectifier to obtain ripple factor in different filters. Characteristics of semiconductor diodes and bipolar transistors. Applications of diodes (for clipping and as ac voltage multiplier). Response of indicating instruments - analogue meter, digital meter, CRO etc. Design of basic DL, TL, DTL, TTL logic gates.

title: Computational Methods
description: Error definition, error propogation, finite difference calculus, interpolation and extrapolation, roots of equations, random number generation, solution of simultaneous linear algebraic equation, least square curve fitting, numerical integration, numerical solution of ordinary differential equations, matrix eigen value problem, topics in linear programming, partial differential equations. Monte Carlo methods, Metropolis algorithm, Simulation.

title: Classical Mechanics I
description: Review of Newton`s law of motion, frames of reference, rotating frames, centrifugal and coriolis forces. Free and constrained motion, D`Alemberts principle and Lagrange`s equation of first kind. Lagrangian formulation, Hamilton342200231s equation of motion. Variational principles. Canonical transformation and Poisson Bracket. Hamilton Jacobi theory and action angle variables.Periodic motion, small oscillations, normal coordinates, Central force, Kepler`s Laws and Rutherford scattering.

title: Waves & Oscillations & Thermodynamics
description: Thermodynamics: Thermal equilibrium, zeroth law and concept of temperature. First law and its consequences, reversible, irreversible and quasi-static processes. Second law: heat engines, concept of entropy and its statistical interpretation. Thermodynamic potentials, Maxwell302222s relations. Joule Kelvin effect. Phase transitions, order of phase transitions, order parameter, critical exponents and the Clausius-Clapeyron equation. Applications to magnetism , superfluidity and superconductivity. Waves & Oscillations :Simple Harmonic motion, damped SHM, critical damping, Sustaining oscillations in a damped oscillator. Driven oscillation, resonance, damped-driven oscillator and its resonance, Q-factor Vanderpol oscillator, non-linear feedback for sustained oscillations.SHM in 2-dim, dependence on initial conditions, Lissajous figures, condition for closed orbits,SHM in 3-dim. Oscillations of two particle systems, symmetric and asymmetric modes, general solution to the problem. Driven oscillations of two particle system.Oscillations of `n` particle systems, normal modes,Formulation of the general problem, eigenvalues and eigenvectors of normal modes, general solution for arbitrary initial conditions. Driven oscillations.Example of a linear triatomic molecule. Longitudinal and transverse oscillations, modding out the zero frequencies. Oscillations of a chain of `n` atoms. Continuum limit,vibrational modes of a string of constant density.Equation of Motion for waves, Standing waves and travelling waves in 1 dimensions. Properties of waves in two and three dimensions Harmonics, Linear superposition of harmonics, odd harmonics, construction of pulse shapesFourier components of a periodic pulse, Fourier analysis and Fourier coefficients.Fourier analysis of arbitrary functions, Fourier Coefficients, Properties of Fourier transform.

title: Quantum Mechanics I
description: Wave functions, superposition principle, wave packets. Schrodinger equation, probability and current densities. Expectation values and Ehrenfest"s theorem. General formalism. Linear vectors and operators in Hilbert space, observables, commuting operators, momentum representations and uncertainty principle. Unitary transformations, Schrodinger and Heisenberg representations, equa-tions of motion. Applications: One dimensional potential problems, linear harmonic oscillator, polynomial solutions, creation and annihilation operators. Central forces, angular momentum, spherical harmon-ics, spin, addition of angular momenta motion in a well, free and bound states in Coulomb potential.

title: Physics Laboratory IV
description: Fresnel"s laws of reflection,Raleigh interferometer, of liquids- Pulfrich"s refractometer, Michelson"s interferometer, Laser diffraction.Geiger Counter, Gama ray spectrometer, Alpha spectrometer,Statisitics of nuclear counting, x-ray emission, x-ray absorption spectrum, x-ray diffraction.

title: Physics Laboratory III
description: Energy gap of semicondutors,Hall effect,Resistivity of ferrites,Transition of ferroelectrics, Hysteris is of wire and rod, Photo conductivity -emission sectrum, -absorption spectrum of iodine Rotation spectrum of cynogen,AlO vibrational spectrum,Gaseous discharge, Densitometry.

title: Electronics Lab III (Microprocessor Lab)
description: Familiarisation of the 8085 kit and programming, data transfer through input/output ports, Analogue to digital conversion using DAC, digital clock, control and transfer of datausing interrupts,control of stepper motor and some electronic instruments demonstration experiments.

title: Electronics Lab. IV
description: Experiments with 8085 based microprocessor kit : Use of Display Routines. 1 second delay using multiloops. Use of the 8255 interface in Modes 0&1. Analogue to Digital Conversion using DAC. 24 Hour Clock. Measurement of OP-Amp characteristics and voltage follower. Inverting and non-inverting amplifier. Sum and difference amplifier. Butterworth Lowpass filter. Integrator and Differentiator. Sine wave oscillators using OP-AMP. Square wave oscillators and Schmitt trigger. Fixed voltage regulator.

title: Statistical Physics
description: 1) Random walk, gaussian statistics and diffusion.2) Statistical ensembles: a)phase space, ergodicity, microcanonical ensemble, Liouville`s theorem, Ideal-gas. b)canonical ensemble, partition function c)grand-canonical ensemble,equivalence of ensembles, 3) Quantum statistical Mechanics: density matrix, Bolzmann,Bose & Fermi statistics.4) Fermi gas: high and low temp limits, electrons in magnetic field, para and diamagnetism.5) Bose gas: black body radiation, phonons, B.E. condensation.

title: Methods in Analytical Techniques
description: Structure and Microstructure analysis by X-ray and electron diffraction, transmission and scanning electron microscopy techniques. Study of molecular structure by resonance techniques like Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR), Fourier transform NMR (FTNMR) and Electron spin resonance (ESR). Study of molecular structure by Infrared (IR), Fourier transform IR (FTIR) and Raman spectroscopies. Study of electronic structure by Photoelectron Spectroscopy and X-ray absorption techniques. Composition analysis by Energy dispersive X-ray (EDX), Auger Electron Spectroscopy (AES) and Secondary ion mass spectrometry (SIMS). Study of surface morphology and structure by Scanning tunneling and Atomic Force microscopies (STM, AFM). Study of magnetic thin films by Ferromagnetic resonance, vibrating sample and torque magnetometry and Magnetic force microscopy.

title: Methods in Experimental Nuclear and Particle Physics
description: Passage of radiation through matter : Interaction of heavy charged particles, neutrons, gamma rays and relativistic particles. Radiation Detection : Detection mechanism, characteristics of detectors. Detectors in Nuclear Physics : gas detectors, scintillation counters, solid state detectors. Detectors in Particle Physics : Drift Chambers, spark chambers, bubble chambers, time projection chambers. Accelerators : Van de Graff, LINAC, Cyclotrons, Synchrotons, Colliders. Pulse Processing : Timing and Energy measurements, data acquisition and analysis. CAMAC and NIM Standards.

title: Applied Solid State Physics
description: Boltzman transport equation, scattering and relaxation time. Optical properties of solids, excitations, concept of plasmons, polarons and polaritons. Dielectric function, dielectric and ferroelectric materials. Band structure of semiconductros, density of states and conductivity effective masses, carrier diffusion processes, excess carrier life time, recombination and trap centres, photo conductivity, electronic properties of surfaces. Dia, para and ferro magnetism, magnetic domains, magnetic materials and application. Magnetic resonance techniques, spin-spin and spin-lattice relaxation. Superconductivity, Meissner effect, tunneling in superconductors, Josephson junctions, squids, superconducting magnets.

title: Analytical Techniques
description: The students will be expected to undertsand the basic principle of working and also carry out analysis of samples in a few simple cases, with the following techniques. Atomic absorption and emission. X-ray diffraction and fluorescence. NMR, ESR, ESCA and Auger. Absorption in visible and infrared. Raman Scattering, Photo-acoustic detection Nitrogen and tunable dye lasers, Electron microscopy

title: Advanced Statistical Mechanics
description: Classical distribution functions, Liouville equation, one particle distribution function, Vlasov equation, Wigner distribution function, Linear Dielectric Response, Boltzmann equation, Transport equations, Hydrodynamic equations. Fluctuations, Brownian motion, Langevin and Fokker-Planck equations, Master equations, Auto-correlation functions. Spectral analysis, Fluctuation-dissipation theorem, Onsager relations. Phase transitions and critical phenomena.

title: Introduction to Nanoscience and Nanotechnology
description: Structure and bonding in Nanoparticles: Electronic structures in Bulk and Nanoclusters. Magic clusters; Magic number, Geometric structures, electronic structures. Theoretical modeling of nanoparticles: Fullerenes, Carbon Nanotubes and Graphenes. Free electrons in Bulk and Nanoparticles. Size and Dimensionality effects: Eelectronic energy bands in semiconductors; electron energy states in quantum confined systems. quantum wires, quantum dots; excitons in semiconductor particles and its size dependence. Absorption and Luminisence in nanoparticles. Electron transport and size scaling in nanoparticles. Size dependence of properties: Introduction to Mie theory with applications to nanoparticles; surface plasmons in noble metal nanoparticles, Magnetic nanoparticles, Superparamagnets. Mechanical properties and size scaling. Nanoparticles in Colloids.

title: Semiconductor Physics
description: An introduction to semiconductors, their crystal structure and their band structure. Intrinsic and extrinsic semiconductors, charge carriers and their effective masses. Methods of electronic structure calculations for semiconductors: plane wave methods, pseudo-potential approaches, semi-empirical pseudopotential method, k.p method, Luttinger Hamiltonian,and the tight-binding approach. Electron-phonon coupling.Optical properties of semiconductors: absorption edges, effective mass approximation, excitons, polaritons. Electron transport properties: high-fieldeffects and magneto-transport.

title: Physics of Nanostructures and nanoscale Devices
description: Electrons in semiconductor hetero-structures: band offsets, effective mass; semiconductor hetero-junctions, 2-DEG systems, quantum wires and quantum dots; Transmission in nanostructures: tunneling in planar barrier, resonant tunnel diodes, Landauer formula, transport in quantum waveguide structures; single electron tunneling and Coulomb blockade. Electron transport in devices: Schottky diodes, p-n junction diodes, short diodes; bipolar and field-effect transistors, MOSFETS, effect of size reduction, short channels; scaling down to nanosizes, FINFETS; issues with nanoscale devices.

title: Introduction to Environmental Science & Engineering
description: History of Environmental Science and Engineering; Biography of Renowned Environmental Scientists and Professionals; Natural Resources; Renewable and Non-renewable Energy Sources; Introduction to Environmental Pollution; Evolution of Pollution Control Strategies and Environmental Infrastructure; Major Environmental Episodes; Evolution of Environmental Acts and Policies; Environmental Ethics; Sustainability Concepts; Recent Research and Future Prospects in the field of Environment.

title: Water and Wastewater Engineering
description: Introduction  to  water  and  wastewater technology; water quality and effluent standards; Water demand forecasting; Determination of reservoir capacity; Water pollution; Environmental hydraulics; Water distribution systems; Wastewater collection; Water and Wastewater treatment: physical, chemical and biological unit operations; Sludge disposal.

title: Environmental Chemistry
description: Aquatic Chemistry, Chemical equilibria and kinetics fundamentals, Acids and bases, Titrations, Acidity, Alkalinity, Buffers and buffer intensity, Chemical equilibrium calculations, pC-pH diagram. Precipitation and dissolution, Water softening and water conditioning, Langelier index, Solubility diagram, Coexistence of phases in equilibrium, Complexation of metal ions and organic complexes in natural water. Oxidation and reduction reactions stoichiometry, Redox couples, pE-pH diagrams, Redox control in natural systems, Basic concepts of organic and colloid chemistry. Soil Chemistry, Weathering reactions, Structure and surface reactions of clays and oxides, Forces at soil-water interfaces. Atmospheric Chemistry, Chemical equilibria and kinetics, Photodissociation and free radical reactions, Chemistry of precipitation, Acid rain.
